topics.title: End Of Life Decisions In Acute Medicine
topics.html:
Related: [[ICU Admission Criteria]], [[Critical Care Monitoring]], [[ABCDE Approach and Initial Assessment]]

[!important]
End-of-life decisions in acute medicine require early, sensitive communication and clear documentation. The goal is to avoid non-beneficial invasive treatment while ensuring comfort and dignity.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise when to shift from curative to palliative goals
  • Apply legal and ethical frameworks (Mental Capacity Act, best interests)
  • Conduct effective goals-of-care conversations
  • Prescribe anticipatory medications for symptom control

2. Triggers for End-of-Life Discussion

  • Cardiac arrest with prolonged resuscitation
  • Multi-organ failure unresponsive to support
  • Advanced dementia / frailty (CFS ≥7) with acute deterioration
  • Known terminal illness with acute complication
  • Patient or family expresses wish to avoid ICU

3. Legal / Ethical Framework (UK)

  • Mental Capacity Act 2005: Assume capacity unless proven otherwise
  • Best interests decision if lacks capacity (involve family, IMCA if no family)
  • DNACPR — does not mean "do not treat"; communicate clearly
  • Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment (ADRT) — legally binding if valid and applicable
  • ReSPECT process — recommended summary plan for emergency care and treatment

4. Communication Structure (REMAP / SPIKES)

  • Review clinical situation
  • Expectations (ask what patient/family understands)
  • Map out goals and values
  • Align treatment with goals
  • Plan (including DNACPR, ceiling of care)

5. Ceiling of Care Documentation

  • Ward level only / Ward + NEWS2 trigger / HDU / Full ICU
  • DNACPR status
  • Specific treatment limits (e.g. no intubation, no vasopressors, no RRT)
  • Review date / trigger for review

6. Anticipatory Prescribing (Last Days of Life)

SymptomFirst-LineAlternative
PainMorphine SCOxycodone / Alfentanil
Agitation / DeliriumMidazolam SCLevomepromazine / Haloperidol
Respiratory secretionsGlycopyrronium / Hyoscine butylbromide SC
NauseaLevomepromazine SCCyclizine / Ondansetron
DyspnoeaMorphine + Midazolam SCOxygen if hypoxic

7. Red Flags

  • Making DNACPR decision without discussion (human rights violation)
  • Assuming "no ICU" = "no treatment"
  • Delaying symptom control

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • DNACPR is specific to CPR — does not limit other treatment
  • Capacity is decision-specific — assess for each decision
  • Best interests = balance benefits, burdens, wishes, beliefs
  • ReSPECT form travels with patient across settings

9. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the difference between DNACPR and ADRT?
  2. How do you assess capacity for a specific decision?
  3. What are the key components of a goals-of-care conversation?
  4. What anticipatory medications would you prescribe for a dying patient?

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • DNACPR does not mean "do not treat"
  • Family cannot demand treatment that is not in best interests
  • Capacity is not global — someone may lack capacity for complex decisions but retain it for simple ones

11. Mnemonics

  • REMAP for goals-of-care conversation structure
  • SPIKES for breaking bad news

12. One-Page Revision Summary

End-of-life in acute medicine:

  • Early recognition + honest conversation
  • DNACPR ≠ no treatment
  • Capacity = decision-specific
  • Best interests if lacks capacity
  • Anticipatory SC meds: morphine, midazolam, glycopyrronium, levomepromazine

13. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • DNACPR scope and communication
  • Capacity assessment (MCA 2005)
  • Best interests framework
  • Anticipatory prescribing (4 key drugs)

Should Know

  • ReSPECT process
  • Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment (ADRT)
  • Legal protection for clinicians acting in best interests

Nice to Know

  • Palliative care referral criteria in acute setting
  • Cultural/religious considerations at end of life

14. MCQs (10)

  1. A DNACPR decision applies to:

A. All medical treatments

B. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation only

C. Intubation and ventilation

D. IV antibiotics and fluids

  1. Under the Mental Capacity Act, capacity is:

A. Global — either present or absent

B. Decision-specific

C. Determined by next of kin

D. Assumed absent in dementia

  1. An Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment (ADRT) is legally binding if:

A. It is verbal

B. It is written, signed, witnessed, and applicable to the situation

C. The family agrees

D. The doctor signs it

  1. First-line anticipatory medication for pain in the dying patient:

A. Paracetamol

B. Morphine SC

C. Ibuprofen

D. Codeine

  1. The ReSPECT process is:

A. A DNACPR form

B. A recommended summary plan for emergency care and treatment

C. An advance directive

D. A palliative care referral form

  1. In a best interests decision, which is NOT a factor?

A. Patient's past wishes

B. Family's wishes for treatment

C. Clinical benefits and burdens

D. Patient's beliefs and values

  1. Glycopyrronium is used for:

A. Pain

B. Agitation

C. Respiratory secretions

D. Nausea

  1. Who can make a DNACPR decision for a patient lacking capacity?

A. Any doctor

B. Senior clinician responsible for care, following best interests process

C. Next of kin

D. Nurse in charge

  1. Which statement about DNACPR is TRUE?

A. It must be reviewed every 24 hours

B. It is legally binding on all healthcare professionals

C. It only applies to CPR, not other treatments

D. It requires court approval

  1. A patient with advanced dementia (CFS 8) develops pneumonia. They have no ADRT. What guides management?

A. Family demand for full treatment

B. Best interests decision involving family and team

C. Automatic DNACPR

D. Mandatory ICU referral

15. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 78-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 30%), CFS 6, admitted with pneumonia. He deteriorates despite ward treatment. Family want "everything done." He lacks capacity for this decision. Best approach:

A. Intubate immediately as family requests

B. Best interests meeting — likely ceiling of care at ward/HDU level with DNACPR

C. Refer to ICU regardless

D. Discharge to hospice

  1. An 85-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic cancer, known ADRT refusing "ventilation and ICU," develops sepsis. She is drowsy but rousable. Management:

A. Ignore ADRT as she is not currently competent

B. Follow ADRT — treat sepsis with antibiotics/fluids but no intubation/ICU

C. Intubate as she hasn't refused antibiotics

D. Call police

  1. A 60-year-old man with MND (motor neurone disease) has a valid ADRT refusing NIV. He presents with respiratory failure. He is conscious and has capacity. He now requests NIV. What do you do?

A. Follow current wishes — provide NIV

B. Refuse NIV per ADRT

C. Seek court order

D. Sedate and intubate

  1. Anticipatory prescribing for a patient dying of lung cancer with noisy breathing and agitation:

A. Morphine + midazolam SC

B. Glycopyrronium + midazolam SC

C. Morphine + glycopyrronium SC

D. Morphine + midazolam + glycopyrronium SC

  1. A patient with DNACPR on ward develops asystole. Nurse calls crash team. Correct action:

A. Full CPR as no senior present

B. Respect DNACPR — confirm death, support family

C. Start CPR until senior arrives

D. Call coroner immediately

  1. Capacity assessment for DNACPR decision in a 70-year-old with delirium:

A. Assume lacks capacity due to delirium

B. Assess specifically for this decision — if fluctuating, wait if possible

C. Ask next of kin

D. Proceed with DNACPR as "best interests"

  1. A patient asks "Am I dying?" — best response:

A. "No, you're getting better"

B. "Why do you ask?" — then honest, compassionate answer

C. "That's for the consultant to say"

D. "We're doing everything we can"

  1. Which is NOT part of ReSPECT?

A. DNACPR recommendation

B. Ceiling of care

C. Organ donation preferences

D. Patient's goals and values

  1. A 55-year-old with severe ARDS on maximal ventilation. Family want continued support. Team feel treatment is futile. Next step:

A. Withdraw ventilation immediately

B. Best interests discussion, seek second opinion, consider ethics committee

C. Continue indefinitely

D. Transfer to another ICU

  1. Levomepromazine is used for:

A. Pain only

B. Nausea and agitation

C. Secretions only

D. Dyspnoea only

16. Flashcards

  • Q: DNACPR scope

A: Applies ONLY to CPR — not to antibiotics, fluids, intubation (unless specified)

  • Q: Capacity test (MCA)

A: Understand → Retain → Weigh → Communicate (decision-specific)

  • Q: Best interests factors

A: Past wishes, beliefs, values, clinical benefits vs burdens, consult family/IMCA

  • Q: First-line anticipatory drugs

A: Morphine (pain/dyspnoea), Midazolam (agitation/seizure), Glycopyrronium (secretions), Levomepromazine (nausea/agitation)

  • Q: ADRT validity

A: Written + signed + witnessed + applicable to current situation

  • Q: ReSPECT

A: Recommended Summary Plan for Emergency Care and Treatment — travels with patient

17. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — DNACPR = CPR only.

MCQ 2: B — Capacity is decision-specific.

MCQ 3: B — ADRT must be written, signed, witnessed, applicable.

MCQ 4: B — Morphine SC first-line for pain/dyspnoea.

MCQ 5: B — ReSPECT = summary plan for emergency care.

MCQ 6: B — Family's wishes are not a best interests factor.

MCQ 7: C — Glycopyrronium for secretions.

MCQ 8: B — Senior clinician responsible for care.

MCQ 9: C — DNACPR only applies to CPR.

MCQ 10: B — Best interests decision.

SBA 1: B — CFS 6 + severe COPD = likely poor ICU outcome; best interests favour ward/HDU ceiling.

SBA 2: B — Valid ADRT applies; treat sepsis but respect limits.

SBA 3: A — Current capacitated wish overrides prior ADRT.

SBA 4: D — All three for dyspnoea + secretions + agitation.

SBA 5: B — Respect DNACPR.

SBA 6: B — Assess specifically; delirium = fluctuating capacity.

SBA 7: B — Honest, compassionate response to direct question.

SBA 8: C — ReSPECT does not include organ donation.

SBA 9: B — Futility disputes need process, not unilateral withdrawal.

SBA 10: B — Levomepromazine for nausea and agitation.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Fluid Resuscitation Strategies
topics.html:
Related: [[Shock - Overview]], [[Inotropes and Vasopressors]], [[Critical Care Monitoring]]

[!important]
Fluids are the first-line resuscitation in most shock types — but type, volume, and speed matter. Over-resuscitation harms.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Choose appropriate fluid type (crystalloids vs colloids vs blood)
  • Apply fluid responsiveness concepts
  • Know static vs dynamic predictors
  • Avoid fluid overload

2. Fluid Types

FluidCompositionIndicationCaution
Balanced crystalloids (Hartmann's, Plasma-Lyte)Near-physiologicalFirst-line for mostLarge volumes → metabolic alkalosis
0.9% SalineNa⁺ 154, Cl⁻ 154Hyperchloraemic acidosis risk; avoid large volumes
Albumin 4.5–5%ColloidSepsis (SSC), large-volume resuscitationCost, no mortality benefit vs crystalloids
Blood productsRBC, FFP, platelets, cryoActive bleeding, Hb <70, coagulopathyTransfusion reactions

3. Fluid Responsiveness

Only ~50% of shocked patients are fluid responders (CO increases >10–15% with fluid bolus).

Static (poor predictors): CVP, PAWP, IVC diameter

Dynamic (good predictors):

  • Passive leg raise (PLR) — temporary preload increase
  • Stroke volume variation (SVV) / Pulse pressure variation (PPV) — need ventilator, sinus rhythm, tidal volume ≥8 mL/kg
  • Mini-fluid challenge (100–250 mL over 1–2 min)

4. Resuscitation Protocol (Sepsis / Undifferentiated Shock)

  1. 500 mL balanced crystalloid over 15 min (or 30 mL/kg over 3h if severe sepsis)
  2. Reassess: MAP, lactate, urine output, clinical perfusion
  3. Repeat if fluid responsive
  4. Stop if no response after 1–2 L → start vasopressors

5. Special Situations

  • Cardiogenic shock: small boluses (250 mL) only if clear hypovolaemia
  • Obstructive shock: avoid fluids (worsens obstruction)
  • Trauma: permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90) until bleeding controlled
  • Burns: Parkland formula (4 mL/kg/%TBSA in 24h, half in first 8h)

6. Complications of Fluid Overload

  • Pulmonary oedema
  • Abdominal compartment syndrome
  • Dilutional coagulopathy
  • Worsened tissue oedema → impaired wound healing, ileus

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Balanced crystalloids preferred over 0.9% saline
  • Dynamic measures (PLR, SVV, PPV) > static (CVP)
  • Fluid responsiveness must be proven — do not blindly bolus
  • Conservative fluid strategy after initial resuscitation improves outcomes

8. Common Viva Questions

  1. Why are balanced crystalloids preferred over normal saline?
  2. What is a dynamic predictor of fluid responsiveness?
  3. How do you manage fluids in cardiogenic shock?

9. One-Page Revision Summary

First-line: Balanced crystalloids (500 mL boluses)

Assess responsiveness: PLR, SVV/PPV, mini-challenge

Stop if unresponsive → vasopressors

Avoid overload — conservative strategy after initial resuscitation

10. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Balanced crystalloids first-line (Hartmann's, Plasma-Lyte)
  • 0.9% saline → hyperchloraemic acidosis, AKI risk
  • Dynamic > static for fluid responsiveness (PLR, SVV, PPV)
  • Stop fluids if unresponsive → vasopressors
  • Permissive hypotension in trauma

Should Know

  • Albumin in sepsis (SSC suggests if large volumes)
  • Blood products: Hb <70 (restrictive)
  • Parkland formula for burns
  • Fluid overload complications (pulmonary oedema, ACS)

Nice to Know

  • Starling curve physiology
  • Colloid vs crystalloid trials (SAFE, CHEST, VISEP)
  • Goal-directed therapy evolution (EGDT → PROCESS/ARISE/PROMISE)

11. MCQs (10)

  1. First-line fluid for sepsis resuscitation (SSC):

A. 0.9% saline

B. Balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's/Plasma-Lyte)

C. Albumin 5%

D. Gelatin

  1. 0.9% saline large volumes cause:

A. Metabolic alkalosis

B. Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis

C. Respiratory acidosis

D. No acid-base disturbance

  1. Best dynamic predictor of fluid responsiveness:

A. CVP

B. PAWP

C. Passive leg raise (PLR)

D. IVC diameter

  1. SVV/PPV require all EXCEPT:

A. Sinus rhythm

B. Mechanical ventilation

C. Tidal volume ≥8 mL/kg

D. Spontaneous breathing

  1. Fluid bolus in cardiogenic shock:

A. 1L rapid

B. 30 mL/kg

C. Small (250 mL) only if clear hypovolaemia

D. Avoid completely

  1. Permissive hypotension target in trauma (uncontrolled bleeding):

A. SBP 60–70

B. SBP 80–90

C. SBP 100–110

D. SBP 120–130

  1. Parkland formula for burns:

A. 2 mL/kg/%TBSA in 24h

B. 4 mL/kg/%TBSA in 24h (half in first 8h)

C. 6 mL/kg/%TBSA in 24h

D. 8 mL/kg/%TBSA in 24h

  1. Blood transfusion threshold in critical care (no active bleed/ischaemia):

A. Hb <70 g/L

B. Hb <80 g/L

C. Hb <90 g/L

D. Hb <100 g/L

  1. Conservative fluid strategy after initial resuscitation:

A. Worsens outcomes

B. Improves outcomes (less pulmonary oedema, shorter ventilation)

C. No difference

D. Increases mortality

  1. Complication of fluid overload:

A. Improved wound healing

B. Pulmonary oedema

C. Increased urine output

D. Better oxygenation

12. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 65-year-old septic patient receives 2L balanced crystalloid. MAP 60, lactate 4.5 (was 4.2). Best next step:

A. Give another 2L

B. Start noradrenaline (fluid unresponsive)

C. Switch to albumin

D. Give furosemide

  1. A cardiogenic shock patient (LVEF 25%, pulmonary oedema) with BP 85/50. Fluid management:

A. 1L saline bolus

B. 30 mL/kg crystalloid

C. 250 mL bolus if clear hypovolaemia only, then vasopressors/inotropes

D. No fluids ever

  1. A trauma patient with suspected pelvic fracture, BP 80/50, HR 130. Fluid strategy:

A. 2L saline rapid

B. Permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90) until bleeding controlled

C. 30 mL/kg crystalloid

D. Albumin 5% 1L

  1. A ventilated septic patient, TV 8 mL/kg, sinus rhythm. SVV 18%. Interpretation:

A. Not fluid responsive

B. Fluid responsive

C. Need CVP

D. Need echo

  1. Why balanced crystalloids over 0.9% saline?

A. Cheaper

B. Lower chloride → less hyperchloraemic acidosis, less AKI

C. Higher sodium

D. Better for hyponatraemia

  1. A 70kg man with 40% TBSA burns. Parkland calculation:

A. 5600 mL in 24h (2800 mL first 8h)

B. 11200 mL in 24h (5600 mL first 8h)

C. 2800 mL in 24h

D. 22400 mL in 24h

  1. In sepsis, if no fluid response after 1–2L:

A. Continue fluids

B. Start vasopressors

C. Give albumin

D. Give blood

  1. Albumin in sepsis (SSC):

A. First-line for all

B. Consider if large-volume resuscitation needed

C. Contraindicated

D. Only in burns

  1. Fluid overload causes all EXCEPT:

A. Pulmonary oedema

B. Abdominal compartment syndrome

C. Dilutional coagulopathy

D. Improved tissue oxygenation

  1. Best initial fluid bolus in undifferentiated shock:

A. 100 mL

B. 250 mL

C. 500 mL balanced crystalloid

D. 1000 mL

13. Flashcards

  • Q: First-line fluid sepsis

A: Balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's/Plasma-Lyte)

  • Q: 0.9% saline risk

A: Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis

  • Q: Dynamic fluid responsiveness

A: PLR, SVV, PPV, mini-challenge

  • Q: Static (poor)

A: CVP, PAWP, IVC diameter

  • Q: Cardiogenic shock fluids

A: 250 mL small boluses only if hypovolaemic

  • Q: Permissive hypotension trauma

A: SBP 80–90 until bleeding controlled

  • Q: Parkland burns

A: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA in 24h, half first 8h

  • Q: Transfusion threshold

A: Hb <70 (restrictive)

  • Q: Conservative fluids after resuscitation

A: Improves outcomes (FACTT, CLASSIC trials)

14. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Balanced crystalloids preferred (SSC).

MCQ 2: B — 0.9% saline Cl⁻ 154 → hyperchloraemic acidosis.

MCQ 3: C — PLR gold standard dynamic.

MCQ 4: D — SVV/PPV require controlled ventilation (not spontaneous).

MCQ 5: C — Small boluses only if hypovolaemic.

MCQ 6: B — SBP 80–90 permissive hypotension.

MCQ 7: B — 4 mL/kg/%TBSA, half first 8h.

MCQ 8: A — Restrictive Hb <70.

MCQ 9: B — Conservative strategy improves outcomes.

MCQ 10: B — Pulmonary oedema from overload.

SBA 1: B — Lactate rising after 2L = fluid unresponsive → vasopressors.

SBA 2: C — Cardiogenic = small bolus only if hypovolaemic, then inotropes/vasopressors.

SBA 3: B — Permissive hypotension in trauma with active bleeding.

SBA 4: B — SVV >13% = fluid responsive.

SBA 5: B — Lower chloride, less AKI, less acidosis.

SBA 5: B — 4 × 70 × 40 = 11200 mL total, 5600 mL first 8h.

SBA 6: B — No response after fluids → vasopressors.

SBA 7: B — Albumin if large volumes needed.

SBA 8: D — Overload impairs tissue oxygenation.

SBA 9: C — 500 mL balanced crystalloid standard bolus.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Hypertensive Emergencies
topics.html:
Related: [[Acute Coronary Syndromes in Critical Care]], [[Cardiogenic Shock]], [[Acute Medicine in Pregnancy]]

[!important]
Hypertensive emergency = SBP >180 + acute target organ damage (TOD): encephalopathy, ICH, ischaemic stroke, MI, ACS, acute LVF, dissection, AKI, eclampsia. Hypertensive urgency = severe HTN (>180/120) WITHOUT TOD. Reduce BP by 20-25% in 1st hour, then to 160/100 over next 2-6 h, then to <140/90 over 24-48 h — NOT too fast (cerebral hypoperfusion). First-line IV drugs: labetalol (most), nicardipine, nitroprusside, nitroglycerin (ACS), urapidil, phentolamine (catecholamine crisis). Specific: Aortic dissection: SBP 100-120 + HR <60 (labetalol + esmolol). Eclampsia: labetalol + magnesium + delivery. Stroke (ischaemic, tPA-eligible): SBP <185, then <180 × 24 h. Stroke (haemorrhagic): SBP <140 within 1 h (INTERACT3, ATACH-2). ACS/Acute LVF: nitroglycerin. Catecholamine crisis: phentolamine. Avoid sublingual nifedipine (unpredictable, stroke risk). Hypertensive urgency = oral treatment, NOT IV.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define hypertensive emergency vs urgency
  • Identify target organ damage (TOD)
  • Apply BP reduction strategy (timing, target)
  • Choose IV antihypertensive based on scenario
  • Manage specific emergencies: dissection, eclampsia, stroke, ACS, catecholamine crisis, encephalopathy
  • Avoid common pitfalls (sublingual nifedipine, rapid drop)
  • Counsel on hypertensive urgency

2. Definition

Hypertensive Emergency

  • SBP >180 mmHg (or DBP >120 mmHg) + acute target organ damage (TOD)
  • TOD: end-organ ischaemia or damage
  • Life-threatening, requires IV therapy in monitored setting

Hypertensive Urgency

  • SBP >180 mmHg (or DBP >120 mmHg) WITHOUT TOD
  • NOT life-threatening
  • Oral therapy, gradual reduction

Malignant Hypertension

  • Severe HTN + papilloedema + retinopathy (grade III/IV) ± AKI
  • Subset of hypertensive emergency
  • Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA)

3. Aetiology

  • Essential hypertension (chronic, undiagnosed/uncontrolled)
  • Renal: renal artery stenosis, glomerulonephritis, polycystic
  • Endocrine: phaeochromocytoma, Cushing's, primary hyperaldosteronism, thyrotoxicosis
  • Drugs: cocaine, amphetamines, MAOI + tyramine, OCP, NSAID, decongestants, sympathomimetics
  • Pregnancy: pre-eclampsia, eclampsia
  • Withdrawal: β-blocker, clonidine, alcohol, methyldopa
  • Aortic dissection
  • CNS: stroke, trauma, raised ICP
  • Burns, severe pain
  • Renal transplant (transplant renal artery stenosis)
  • Autonomic dysreflexia (spinal cord injury)

4. Pathophysiology

  • Acute BP rise → exceeds autoregulation → endothelial injury
  • Fibrinoid necrosis of arterioles
  • Ischaemia of brain (encephalopathy), heart (ACS), kidneys (AKI), retina
  • Catecholamine surge (sympathetic activation)
  • RAAS activation → vasoconstriction, sodium retention

5. Clinical Features (TOD)

Neurological

  • Hypertensive encephalopathy (headache, confusion, seizures, visual disturbance, papilloedema)
  • Stroke (ischaemic, haemorrhagic, TIA)
  • Subarachnoid haemorrhage

Cardiovascular

  • Acute LV failure / pulmonary oedema
  • Acute coronary syndrome (MI, unstable angina)
  • Aortic dissection
  • Aortic aneurysm rupture

Renal

  • Acute kidney injury (AKI)
  • Haematuria, proteinuria
  • Acute renal failure

Other

  • Retinopathy (grade III/IV, papilloedema, exudates, haemorrhages)
  • Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA)
  • DIC

6. Investigations

Urgent

  • Bloods: FBC, U&E, creatinine, glucose, urate, TSH
  • FBC with film (schistocytes, MAHA)
  • Urinalysis (proteinuria, haematuria)
  • Cardiac: ECG (LVH, ischaemia), troponin
  • CXR (cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, widened mediastinum for dissection)
  • CT head (encephalopathy, bleed, stroke)
  • CT aortogram (dissection)
  • Echocardiography (LV function, regional wall motion)

Specific

  • Renal ultrasound, Doppler (renal artery stenosis)
  • Plasma metanephrines, urinary VMA (phaeochromocytoma)
  • Pregnancy test (eclampsia)
  • Toxicology screen (cocaine, amphetamines)
  • Aldosterone, renin (Conn's syndrome)

7. Management Principles

General

  • Monitored bed (HDU/ICU)
  • IV access × 2
  • Arterial line for continuous BP
  • Urinary catheter (urine output)
  • Treat pain, anxiety, hypoxia (sympathetic)
  • Identify + treat cause
  • Don't drop too fast (cerebral hypoperfusion)

BP Reduction Targets

PhaseTarget
First hour↓ 20-25% from baseline
2-6 hours<160/100 mmHg
24-48 hours<140/90 mmHg (chronic levels)
Aortic dissectionSBP 100-120 + HR <60 (rapidly, within 20 min)
Stroke (ischaemic, tPA)SBP <185 (pre-tPA), then <180 × 24 h
Stroke (haemorrhagic)SBP <140 within 1 h
Eclampsia<160/110, then MAP 80-100
Catecholamine crisisgradual (avoid hypotension)

Why Not Too Fast?

  • Cerebral autoregulation shifts right in chronic HTN
  • Rapid drop → watershed cerebral infarcts, stroke, blindness
  • Aim: gradual reduction over hours to days

8. IV Antihypertensive Drugs

DrugDoseOnsetUse
Labetalol10-20 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg/min infusion OR 20 mg q5min5-10 minFirst-line; most scenarios; pregnancy-safe; asthma (β-blocker)
Nicardipine5-15 mg/h IV infusion5-10 minMost scenarios; post-op; avoid in acute LVF
Sodium nitroprusside0.5-10 mcg/kg/min IV infusion<1 minMost severe; monitor cyanide toxicity; not first-line
Nitroglycerin (GTN)5-200 mcg/min IV infusion2-5 minACS, acute LVF; not great for cerebral
Hydralazine5-10 mg IV q20min10-20 minPregnancy (eclampsia); reflex tachycardia
Phentolamine2.5-5 mg IV q5-15min1-2 minCatecholamine crisis (phaeo, MAOI)
Esmolol500 mcg/kg bolus, then 50-200 mcg/kg/min1-2 minAortic dissection (rate control); perioperative
Urapidil10-50 mg IV bolus, then 5-40 mg/h5 minEurope; alternative to labetalol
Clevidipine1-32 mg/h IV1-2 minPerioperative

Drug Choice by Scenario

ScenarioFirst-line
Most emergenciesLabetalol or nicardipine
Aortic dissectionLabetalol + esmolol (rate + BP)
Acute coronary syndromeNitroglycerin (GTN) + β-blocker
Acute LVF / pulmonary oedemaNitroglycerin + diuretics, ± labetalol
EclampsiaLabetalol + magnesium + delivery
Stroke (ischaemic, tPA)Labetalol (SBP <185)
Stroke (haemorrhagic)Labetalol (SBP <140)
Catecholamine crisisPhentolamine ± β-blocker
EncephalopathyLabetalol or nicardipine
Renal failureLabetalol, nicardipine, urapidil (avoid nitroprusside)
PregnancyLabetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine
Post-opClevidipine, nicardipine

9. Specific Emergencies

Aortic Dissection

  • SBP 100-120 + HR <60 (rapidly, within 20-30 min)
  • IV β-blocker FIRST: esmolol or labetalol (reduce dP/dt)
  • Then vasodilator (nicardipine or nitroprusside)
  • Pain control (morphine)
  • Surgical consultation for Type A
  • Type B: medical management, β-blocker + CCB

Hypertensive Encephalopathy

  • Exclude: stroke, bleed, mass (CT head first)
  • IV labetalol or nicardipine
  • 20-25% reduction in MAP in first hour
  • Seizure control (lorazepam, levetiracetam)
  • Avoid nitroprusside (↑ICP)

Stroke (Ischaemic)

  • Not tPA: lower BP only if >220/120 (for tPA-eligible, <185/110)
  • tPA candidate: SBP <185, then <180 × 24 h
  • Mechanical thrombectomy: similar BP targets
  • IV labetalol, nicardipine
  • AVOID rapid drop (worsen ischaemia)
  • Permissive HTN if not thrombolysed (up to 220/120)

Stroke (Haemorrhagic)

  • INTERACT3, ATACH-2: SBP <140 within 1-2 h
  • Reduces haematoma expansion
  • IV labetalol, nicardipine, urapidil
  • Reversal of anticoagulation if needed
  • Neurosurgery referral

Acute Coronary Syndrome

  • GTN (nitroglycerin) IV + β-blocker (metoprolol IV)
  • Aspirin, heparin, P2Y12, statin per ACS protocol
  • PCI if STEMI

Acute LVF / Pulmonary Oedema

  • GTN IV + diuretics (furosemide)
  • CPAP/BiPAP
  • ± Morphine
  • Labetalol if HTN coexists

Acute Kidney Injury

  • Treat cause
  • IV labetalol, nicardipine
  • Avoid ACE-i/ARB in AKI
  • Dobutamine if cardiogenic component

Eclampsia (see [[Acute Medicine in Pregnancy]])

  • MgSO₄ 4 g IV over 5 min (load), then 1 g/h infusion
  • BP control: labetalol (first-line) or hydralazine or nifedipine
  • Delivery definitive treatment
  • HELLP syndrome: deliver + supportive

Catecholamine Crisis (Phaeochromocytoma, MAOI)

  • α-blockade FIRST: phentolamine 2.5-5 mg IV OR phenoxybenzamine
  • Then β-blocker (esmolol/labetalol) — NEVER β alone (unopposed α)
  • Avoid: morphine (histamine), metoclopramide, glucagon
  • Pre-op preparation: α-blockade × 10-14 days, then β

Sympathomimetic Toxidrome (Cocaine, Amphetamine)

  • Benzodiazepines (first-line): lorazepam, diazepam
  • Nitroglycerin (CCB controversial in cocaine: ↑CNS toxicity)
  • Phentolamine for severe HTN
  • AVOID β-blockers (unopposed α)

Autonomic Dysreflexia (SCI)

  • Sit up + remove tight clothing + empty bladder
  • Nifedipine SL (or chew)
  • Treat cause: full bladder, constipation, noxious stimulus

10. Hypertensive Urgency

  • NOT life-threatening
  • Oral therapy: amlodipine, losartan, atenolol, etc.
  • Reduce over 24-48 h to <160/100
  • Outpatient if no TOD
  • AVOID IV therapy (rapid drop → stroke)

11. Complications of Therapy

  • Cerebral hypoperfusion (rapid drop)
  • Ischaemic stroke
  • Retinal infarction
  • Myocardial ischaemia
  • Rebound HTN (clonidine, β-blocker withdrawal)
  • Cyanide toxicity (nitroprusside >4 mcg/kg/min >24 h)

12. Contraindicated Drugs

  • Sublingual nifedipine (unpredictable absorption, stroke risk)
  • β-blocker in catecholamine crisis (unopposed α)
  • Nitroprusside in renal failure (cyanide)
  • Nitroprusside in pregnancy (cyanide crosses placenta)

13. Prognosis

  • Without treatment: 1-year mortality 90% in malignant HTN
  • With treatment: 5-year survival 60-80%
  • TOD improves with BP control (some, e.g., LVH)
  • Aortic dissection: 1-month mortality 50% (Type A) or 10% (Type B)

14. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  1. Emergency = SBP >180 + TOD (vs urgency = no TOD)
  2. Reduction target: ↓ 20-25% in 1st hour, <160/100 in 6 h, <140/90 in 24-48 h
  3. Most emergencies: labetalol or nicardipine
  4. Aortic dissection: SBP 100-120 + HR <60 (β-blocker first: esmolol/labetalol)
  5. Eclampsia: labetalol + MgSO₄ + delivery
  6. Stroke (ischaemic, tPA): SBP <185
  7. Stroke (haemorrhagic): SBP <140 within 1 h
  8. ACS/Acute LVF: GTN
  9. Catecholamine crisis: phentolamine (α first, then β)
  10. Cocaine/amphetamines: benzodiazepines, AVOID β-blockers
  11. Sublingual nifedipine: AVOID (unpredictable)
  12. Malignant HTN = papilloedema + retinopathy + AKI + MAHA
  13. Avoid rapid drop (cerebral hypoperfusion)
  14. Hypertensive urgency: oral Rx, NOT IV
  15. Nitroprusside: monitor cyanide (>4 mcg/kg/min >24 h)

15. Common Viva Questions

  1. Hypertensive emergency vs urgency
  2. BP reduction targets
  3. First-line drugs
  4. Aortic dissection management
  5. Eclampsia management
  6. Stroke BP targets
  7. Catecholamine crisis
  8. Cocaine HTN
  9. Why avoid sublingual nifedipine
  10. Malignant hypertension

16. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Emergency = TOD present, not just BP level
  • Avoid rapid drop (cerebral hypoperfusion)
  • β-blocker alone in catecholamine crisis = unopposed α
  • Sublingual nifedipine = dangerous (unpredictable)
  • Aortic dissection = β-blocker first (esmolol/labetalol), then vasodilator
  • Nitroprusside in renal failure = cyanide toxicity
  • Encephalopathy: exclude stroke/bleed first
  • Stroke ischaemic: permissive HTN (220/120) unless tPA
  • Stroke haemorrhagic: SBP <140 (INTERACT3)
  • Cocaine: benzodiazepines, not β-blockers
  • Eclampsia: MgSO₄ (NOT diazepam/phenytoin for seizure)
  • Malignant HTN: MAHA + AKI + retinopathy
  • Hypertensive urgency: oral, NOT IV

17. Mnemonics

  • Emergency = TOD, Urgency = no TOD
  • BP targets: 20-25% in 1 h, <160/100 in 6 h, <140/90 in 24-48 h
  • Most: Labetalol or Nicardipine
  • Aortic dissection: SBP 100-120 + HR <60 (β first)
  • Eclampsia: Labetalol + MgSO₄ + Delivery
  • Stroke (isch, tPA): SBP <185
  • Stroke (haem): SBP <140
  • ACS/LVF: GTN
  • Catecholamine crisis: α first (phentolamine)
  • Cocaine: Benzos, NO β-blocker
  • Malignant HTN: MAHA + AKI + retinopathy
  • AVOID: sublingual nifedipine
  • Nitroprusside: monitor cyanide

18. Mind Map
