19. Flowchart — Hypertensive Emergency

```mermaid

flowchart TD

A[BP >180] --> B{TOD present?}

B -->|No| C[Urgency: oral, gradual, outpatient]

B -->|Yes| D[Monitored bed, arterial line]

D --> E{Scenario?}

E -->|Dissection| F[Esmolol/labetalol FIRST, then vasodilator, SBP 100-120]

E -->|ACS/LVF| G[GTN + diuretic]

E -->|Eclampsia| H[Labetalol + MgSO4 + delivery]

E -->|Stroke haem| I[SBP <140 within 1h]

E -->|Stroke isch tPA| J[SBP <185]

E -->|Catecholamine crisis| K[Phentolamine alpha first]

E -->|Cocaine| L[Benzos, NO beta]

E -->|Encephalopathy| M[Labetalol/nicardipine]

E -->|Other| N[Labetalol/nicardipine]

F --> O[20-25% reduction in 1h]

G --> O

H --> O

I --> O

J --> O

K --> O

L --> O

M --> O

N --> O

O --> P[Target <160/100 in 6h]

P --> Q[Target <140/90 in 24-48h]

```

20. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Emergency = SBP >180 + TOD; urgency = no TOD (oral Rx)
  • Reduction: 20-25% in 1st hour, <160/100 in 6 h, <140/90 in 24-48 h
  • Most emergencies: labetalol or nicardipine
  • Aortic dissection: SBP 100-120 + HR <60 (β-blocker first)
  • Eclampsia: labetalol + MgSO₄ + delivery
  • Stroke ischaemic (tPA): SBP <185; haemorrhagic: SBP <140 within 1 h
  • ACS/LVF: GTN + β-blocker
  • Catecholamine crisis: α first (phentolamine), then β
  • Cocaine: benzos, AVOID β-blockers
  • Malignant HTN: MAHA + AKI + retinopathy
  • AVOID sublingual nifedipine (unpredictable)
  • Avoid rapid drop (cerebral hypoperfusion)
  • Nitroprusside: monitor cyanide (>4 mcg/kg/min >24 h)
  • Hypertensive urgency: oral, NOT IV

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • Define hypertensive emergency vs urgency
  • State BP reduction targets
  • Outline aortic dissection management
  • Describe eclampsia management
  • List stroke BP targets
  • Outline catecholamine crisis Rx
  • State cocaine HTN management
  • List contraindications

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

21. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Emergency vs urgency
  • BP reduction targets
  • Labetalol/nicardipine first-line
  • Aortic dissection (SBP 100-120, HR <60)
  • Eclampsia (labetalol + MgSO₄)
  • Stroke BP targets (ischaemic, haemorrhagic)
  • ACS/LVF (GTN)
  • Catecholamine crisis (α first)
  • Cocaine (benzos, no β)
  • Malignant HTN
  • AVOID sublingual nifedipine
  • Avoid rapid drop

Should Know

  • Hydralazine in pregnancy
  • Nitroprusside (cyanide)
  • Esmolol (rate control)
  • Autonomic dysreflexia
  • Phentolamine dose
  • HELLP syndrome
  • Phaeochromocytoma pre-op
  • Drug choice by scenario
  • Sympathomimetic toxidrome
  • Hypertensive urgency Rx

Nice to Know

  • INTERACT3, ATACH-2 trials
  • Clevidipine
  • Cyanide toxicity
  • Rebound HTN
  • Nitroprusside monitoring
  • Watershed infarcts
  • Urapidil
  • Renal failure dosing
  • MAHA
  • ESRD/HD patients

22. Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

23. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • Definitions (emergency vs urgency vs malignant)
  • Pathophysiology
  • Causes
  • Clinical features (TOD)
  • Investigations
  • BP reduction targets (timing, %)
  • IV antihypertensive drugs (table)
  • Specific emergencies:
  • Aortic dissection
  • Eclampsia
  • Stroke (ischaemic + haemorrhagic)
  • ACS / LVF
  • Catecholamine crisis
  • Cocaine
  • Encephalopathy
  • AKI
  • Avoid pitfalls (sublingual nifedipine, rapid drop, β alone in phaeo)
  • Hypertensive urgency (oral, not IV)

Short Note Skeleton

  • Hypertensive emergency Rx
  • Aortic dissection HTN Rx
  • Eclampsia HTN Rx
  • Stroke BP targets
  • Catecholamine crisis Rx
  • Malignant HTN

Viva One-Liners

  • "Emergency = SBP >180 + TOD; urgency = no TOD"
  • "Reduce 20-25% in 1st hour, <160/100 in 6 h, <140/90 in 24-48 h"
  • "Labetalol or nicardipine first-line for most"
  • "Aortic dissection: SBP 100-120, HR <60, β-blocker first"
  • "Eclampsia: labetalol + MgSO₄ + delivery"
  • "Stroke ischaemic (tPA): SBP <185; haemorrhagic: SBP <140"
  • "ACS/LVF: GTN + β-blocker"
  • "Catecholamine crisis: α first (phentolamine), then β"
  • "Cocaine: benzos, NO β-blockers"
  • "Malignant HTN: MAHA + AKI + retinopathy"
  • "AVOID sublingual nifedipine"
  • "Hypertensive urgency: oral, NOT IV"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 60-year-old, SBP 220, headache, papilloedema → encephalopathy → IV labetalol, monitor
  • Aortic dissection, SBP 220, HR 110 → esmolol + labetalol, target SBP 100-120
  • Eclampsia 32 weeks, BP 170/110, seizures → MgSO₄ + labetalol + delivery
  • Stroke ICH, SBP 180 → SBP <140 within 1 h, labetalol
  • Cocaine chest pain + HTN → benzos, GTN, avoid β
  • Phaeochromocytoma intra-op, BP 240/130 → phentolamine
  • Hypertensive urgency SBP 180, asymptomatic → oral amlodipine, gradual, outpatient

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • Emergency = TOD, urgency = no TOD
  • Reduce 20-25% in 1h
  • Most: labetalol/nicardipine
  • Dissection: SBP 100-120, HR <60
  • Eclampsia: labetalol + MgSO₄ + delivery
  • Stroke isch (tPA): SBP <185
  • Stroke haem: SBP <140
  • ACS/LVF: GTN
  • Catecholamine: α first
  • Cocaine: benzos, no β
  • Malignant: MAHA + AKI
  • NO sublingual nifedipine
  • NO rapid drop
  • Nitroprusside → cyanide

24. Summary

Hypertensive emergency = SBP >180 + acute target organ damage (TOD); urgency = severe HTN without TOD (oral Rx, gradual). TOD: encephalopathy, stroke (ischaemic/haemorrhagic), ACS, acute LVF, aortic dissection, AKI, retinopathy (grade III/IV), MAHA. Malignant HTN: severe HTN + papilloedema + retinopathy + AKI + MAHA. BP reduction targets: ↓ 20-25% in 1st hour → <160/100 in 6 h → <140/90 in 24-48 h. Avoid rapid drop (cerebral hypoperfusion → watershed infarcts). First-line IV drugs: labetalol (10-20 mg bolus, 1-2 mg/min infusion; first-line in most + pregnancy), nicardipine (5-15 mg/h; most scenarios), GTN (5-200 mcg/min; ACS/LVF), esmolol (dissection), phentolamine (catecholamine crisis), hydralazine (pregnancy alt), nitroprusside (most severe; monitor cyanide >4 mcg/kg/min >24 h). Specific emergencies: Aortic dissection → SBP 100-120 + HR <60 (β-blocker first: esmolol/labetalol, then vasodilator). Eclampsia → labetalol + MgSO₄ 4 g IV load + 1 g/h + delivery. Stroke ischaemic (tPA-eligible) → SBP <185 (pre), then <180 × 24 h; non-tPA → permissive HTN up to 220/120. Stroke haemorrhagic → SBP <140 within 1 h (INTERACT3, ATACH-2). ACS/LVF → GTN + β-blocker. Catecholamine crisis (phaeo, MAOI) → α-blockade FIRST (phentolamine 2.5-5 mg IV), then β. Cocaine/amphetamines → benzodiazepines (NOT β-blockers — unopposed α). Autonomic dysreflexia (SCI) → sit up + empty bladder + nifedipine SL. Hypertensive urgency: oral therapy (amlodipine, losartan, atenolol), gradual reduction, NOT IV (rapid drop → stroke). AVOID sublingual nifedipine (unpredictable absorption, stroke risk). AVOID rapid drop, β-blocker alone in phaeo, nitroprusside in renal failure/pregnancy (cyanide). Prognosis: 1-year mortality 90% untreated malignant HTN, 5-yr survival 60-80% treated.

25. MCQs (10)

  1. Hypertensive emergency defined as:

A. SBP >180

B. SBP >180 + acute target organ damage

C. DBP >100

D. Malignant HTN

  1. First-line IV drug in most hypertensive emergencies:

A. Sodium nitroprusside

B. Labetalol or nicardipine

C. Hydralazine

D. GTN

  1. Aortic dissection BP target:

A. <140/90

B. <160/100

C. SBP 100-120 + HR <60

D. <120/80

  1. Eclampsia treatment includes:

A. Diazepam

B. Phenytoin

C. MgSO₄ + labetalol + delivery

D. β-blocker only

  1. Catecholamine crisis first drug:

A. β-blocker

B. α-blocker (phentolamine)

C. Calcium channel blocker

D. ACE inhibitor

  1. Cocaine-induced HTN first-line:

A. β-blocker

B. Benzodiazepine

C. Nitroprusside

D. Phentolamine

  1. Stroke (ischaemic, tPA-eligible) SBP target:

A. <140

B. <185

C. <220

D. <120

  1. Stroke (haemorrhagic) SBP target:

A. <180

B. <140 within 1 h

C. <220

D. <120

  1. Sublingual nifedipine in hypertensive emergency:

A. First-line

B. Safe

C. AVOIDED (unpredictable, stroke risk)

D. Cheap and effective

  1. Malignant hypertension features include all EXCEPT:

A. Papilloedema

B. Grade IV retinopathy

C. Bradycardia

D. MAHA

26. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 60-year-old, SBP 220, headache, papilloedema, no focal deficit, AKI. Diagnosis:

A. Hypertensive urgency

B. Hypertensive emergency (encephalopathy + AKI)

C. Stroke

D. Migraine

  1. Aortic dissection, SBP 220, HR 110. First drug:

A. Nitroprusside

B. Esmolol or labetalol (β-blocker first)

C. Hydralazine

D. GTN

  1. Eclampsia, 32 weeks, BP 170/110, seizures. First-line seizure Rx:

A. Diazepam

B. Phenytoin

C. MgSO₄ 4 g IV over 5 min

D. Levetiracetam

  1. ICH stroke, SBP 180. Target:

A. <180

B. <140 within 1 h (INTERACT3)

C. <220

D. <120

  1. Phaeochromocytoma, BP 240/130 intra-op. First drug:

A. Esmolol

B. Phentolamine (α first)

C. Nitroprusside

D. Labetalol

  1. Cocaine chest pain, BP 180/110, SOB. Rx:

A. β-blocker

B. Benzodiazepine + GTN

C. Aspirin

D. Morphine

  1. Hypertensive urgency, SBP 180, asymptomatic. Rx:

A. IV labetalol

B. Oral amlodipine, gradual reduction, outpatient

C. Sublingual nifedipine

D. Admit + IV

  1. Nitroprusside >4 mcg/kg/min for >24 h risk:

A. Methaemoglobinaemia

B. Cyanide toxicity

C. HyperK

D. Rhabdomyolysis

  1. Autonomic dysreflexia, SCI, BP 200/120. First action:

A. IV hydralazine

B. Sit up + remove tight clothing + empty bladder + nifedipine

C. Intubate

D. Anaesthesia

  1. ESRD patient, hypertensive emergency. Drug to avoid:

A. Labetalol

B. Nicardipine

C. Nitroprusside (cyanide accumulation)

D. Urapidil

27. Flashcards

  • Q: Hypertensive emergency definition

A: SBP >180 + acute TOD

  • Q: BP reduction targets

A: 20-25% in 1h, <160/100 in 6h, <140/90 in 24-48h

  • Q: First-line IV drugs

A: Labetalol or nicardipine

  • Q: Aortic dissection target

A: SBP 100-120 + HR <60

  • Q: Aortic dissection first drug

A: Esmolol/labetalol (β first)

  • Q: Eclampsia Rx

A: Labetalol + MgSO4 + delivery

  • Q: MgSO4 dose

A: 4 g IV load, then 1 g/h

  • Q: Catecholamine crisis first drug

A: Phentolamine (α first)

  • Q: Cocaine HTN Rx

A: Benzodiazepine

  • Q: Stroke (isch, tPA) SBP target

A: <185

  • Q: Stroke (haem) SBP target

A: <140

  • Q: Malignant HTN features

A: Papilloedema + retinopathy + AKI + MAHA

  • Q: AVOID drug

A: Sublingual nifedipine

  • Q: Hypertensive urgency Rx

A: Oral, gradual, outpatient

28. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Emergency = SBP >180 + TOD.

MCQ 2: B — Labetalol or nicardipine first-line.

MCQ 3: C — Dissection: SBP 100-120 + HR <60.

MCQ 4: C — MgSO₄ + labetalol + delivery.

MCQ 5: B — α-blocker (phentolamine) first in catecholamine crisis.

MCQ 6: B — Benzodiazepine in cocaine.

MCQ 7: B — Stroke isch (tPA): SBP <185.

MCQ 8: B — Stroke haem: SBP <140.

MCQ 9: C — Sublingual nifedipine avoided.

MCQ 10: C — Bradycardia is not a feature of malignant HTN.

SBA 1: B — Emergency (TOD = encephalopathy + AKI).

SBA 2: B — β-blocker first in dissection.

SBA 3: C — MgSO₄ 4 g IV.

SBA 4: B — ICH: SBP <140 within 1 h.

SBA 5: B — Phentolamine (α first) in phaeo.

SBA 6: B — Benzos + GTN in cocaine.

SBA 7: B — Oral, gradual, outpatient.

SBA 8: B — Nitroprusside >24 h = cyanide.

SBA 9: B — Sit up + empty bladder + nifedipine.

SBA 10: C — Nitroprusside avoided in ESRD.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-15

topics.title: Hypovolaemic Shock
topics.html:
Related: [[Shock - Overview]], [[Cardiogenic Shock]], [[Obstructive Shock]]

[!important]
Hypovolaemic shock = inadequate circulating blood volume leading to reduced preload, cardiac output, and tissue perfusion. Most common cause of shock worldwide.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Identify causes of hypovolaemic shock
  • Differentiate haemorrhagic vs non-haemorrhagic hypovolaemia
  • Apply ATLS principles of fluid resuscitation
  • Recognise when blood products are needed

2. Classification by Volume Loss

ClassBlood LossPulseBPUrine OutputMental Status
I<750 mL<100Normal>30 mL/hrNormal
II750–1500100–120Normal20–30 mL/hrMild anxiety
III1500–2000120–140Decreased5–15 mL/hrConfused
IV>2000>140DecreasedMinimalLethargic

3. Causes

Haemorrhagic

  • Trauma (external + internal)
  • GI bleed, ruptured AAA, ectopic pregnancy

Non-haemorrhagic

  • Severe dehydration (diarrhoea, vomiting, burns, DKA, diabetes insipidus)

4. Clinical Features

  • Cool, clammy skin, delayed capillary refill
  • Tachycardia, narrow pulse pressure
  • Flat neck veins, low JVP
  • Oliguria, confusion

5. Management

  • Stop the bleeding (pressure, tourniquet, surgery, endoscopy)
  • Two large-bore IV cannulae
  • Crystalloid resuscitation (balanced solutions)
  • Blood products early if Class III/IV or ongoing bleeding (1:1:1 ratio)
  • Permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90 mmHg) in penetrating trauma until bleeding controlled
  • Monitor lactate, base deficit, urine output

6. Red Flags

  • Class III/IV shock
  • Ongoing bleeding
  • Coagulopathy / trauma-induced coagulopathy

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Tachycardia may be absent in elderly or beta-blocked patients
  • Hypotension is a late sign
  • Give blood early in haemorrhagic shock — crystalloids alone worsen coagulopathy

8. Common Viva Questions

  1. Classify hypovolaemic shock by blood loss.
  2. What is permissive hypotension and when is it used?
  3. When would you activate massive transfusion protocol?

9. One-Page Revision Summary

Hypovolaemia = low preload

Stop bleeding + large-bore IV access + balanced crystalloids

Early blood in significant haemorrhage

Monitor lactate and urine output

10. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • ATLS classification I–IV by blood loss (pulse, BP, UO, mental status)
  • Haemorrhagic vs non-haemorrhagic causes
  • Stop bleeding first, then 2 large-bore IV + balanced crystalloids
  • Early blood products (1:1:1) for Class III/IV or ongoing bleed
  • Permissive hypotension SBP 80–90 in penetrating trauma

Should Know

  • Lactate, base deficit, UO as resuscitation targets
  • Trauma-induced coagulopathy (crystalloids dilute clotting factors)
  • Tranexamic acid within 3 hours of trauma

Nice to Know

  • Damage control resuscitation vs surgery
  • Cell salvage, REBOA
  • Permissive hypotension contraindicated in TBI

11. MCQs (10)

  1. ATLS Class III haemorrhage blood loss:

A. <750 mL

B. 750–1500 mL

C. 1500–2000 mL

D. >2000 mL

  1. ATLS Class IV — pulse:

A. <100

B. 100–120

C. 120–140

D. >140

  1. Permissive hypotension target SBP in penetrating trauma:

A. 60–70

B. 80–90

C. 100–110

D. 120–130

  1. Massive transfusion protocol ratio (RBC:FFP:Platelets):

A. 1:1:2

B. 1:1:1

C. 2:1:1

D. 1:2:1

  1. Hypovolaemic shock from dehydration — fluid of choice:

A. Albumin

B. Balanced crystalloids (Hartmann's/Plasma-Lyte)

C. 0.9% saline only

D. Gelatin

  1. Flat neck veins + low JVP in hypovolaemic shock:

A. Normal finding

B. Key differentiating sign from cardiogenic

C. Only in severe shock

D. Not reliable

  1. Tranexamic acid in trauma — timing:

A. Within 1 hour

B. Within 3 hours of injury

C. Within 6 hours

D. Only in theatre

  1. Crystalloids alone in major haemorrhage worsen:

A. Hypertension

B. Coagulopathy (dilutional)

C. Tachycardia

D. Hypothermia only

  1. Urine output target in resuscitation:

A. >30 mL/hr

B. >0.5 mL/kg/hr

C. >1 mL/kg/hr

D. >2 mL/kg/hr

  1. Hypotension in hypovolaemic shock is a:

A. Early sign

B. Late sign (compensated until Class III)

C. Only in Class IV

D. Not seen

12. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old stab wound abdomen, BP 85/50, HR 130, cool peripheries, Hb 80 g/L. FAST positive. Management:

A. 2L crystalloid then CT

B. Blood products early (1:1:1) + urgent laparotomy (permissive hypotension)

C. Noradrenaline + CT

D. Observe

  1. A 70-year-old on warfarin, GI bleed, BP 80/50, Hb 65, INR 4.5. Fluid/blood:

A. Crystalloid 2L

B. Packed RBCs + FFP (reverse warfarin) + PCC/4F-PCC

C. Platelets only

D. Albumin

  1. A 25-year-old trauma, pelvic fracture, SBP 70. Pelvic binder applied. Resuscitation:

A. Aggressive crystalloid to SBP >110

B. Permissive hypotension SBP 80–90 + blood products + pelvic packing/angio

C. Noradrenaline to SBP >100

D. 3L crystalloid

  1. ATLS Class II haemorrhage (750–1500 mL) — typical pulse:

A. <100

B. 100–120

C. 120–140

D. >140

  1. A patient with DKA, severe dehydration, HR 120, BP 90/60. Fluid:

A. 0.9% saline 1L bolus then balanced

B. Balanced crystalloids (0.9% saline initially if severe hyponatraemia)

C. Albumin

D. Blood

  1. Lactate clearance target in shock resuscitation:

A. 5% per hour

B. ≥10–20% per 2 hours

C. Normalise in 1 hour

D. <1 mmol/L in 6 hours

  1. Base deficit >6 in trauma indicates:

A. Mild shock

B. Significant hypoperfusion / occult shock

C. Normal

D. Respiratory alkalosis

  1. A 40-year-old postpartum haemorrhage, ongoing bleed, BP 70/40. TXA:

A. Not indicated

B. 1g IV within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial)

C. Only if fibrinogen low

D. After hysterectomy

  1. Hypovolaemic shock vs cardiogenic — flat neck veins means:

A. Cardiogenic

B. Hypovolaemic (low preload)

C. Both

D. Neither

  1. Two large-bore IV cannulae in trauma — size:

A. 18G

B. 14–16G (or intraosseous)

C. 20G

C. 22G

13. Flashcards

  • Q: ATLS Class I–IV

A: I<750, II 750–1500, III 1500–2000, IV>2000 mL

  • Q: Permissive hypotension

A: SBP 80–90 in penetrating trauma until bleeding controlled

  • Q: Massive transfusion ratio

A: 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

  • Q: Flat neck veins =

A: Hypovolaemic (differentiates from cardiogenic)

  • Q: TXA in trauma

A: 1g IV within 3 hours

  • Q: Lactate clearance

A: ≥10–20% per 2 hours

  • Q: Blood early in haemorrhagic shock

A: Crystalloids worsen coagulopathy

  • Q: Hypotension = late sign

A: Compensated until Class III

14. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: C — Class III = 1500–2000 mL.

MCQ 2: D — Class IV pulse >140.

MCQ 3: B — SBP 80–90 permissive hypotension.

MCQ 4: B — 1:1:1 ratio.

MCQ 5: B — Balanced crystalloids preferred.

MCQ 6: B — Low JVP = hypovolaemic; high JVP = cardiogenic.

MCQ 7: B — TXA within 3 hours (CRASH-2).

MCQ 8: B — Crystalloids dilute clotting factors.

MCQ 9: B — UO >0.5 mL/kg/hr.

MCQ 10: B — Hypotension is late (compensated).

SBA 1: B — FAST positive + shock = urgent laparotomy + blood (1:1:1) + permissive hypotension.

SBA 2: B — Warfarin bleed = RBC + FFP + PCC/4F-PCC for INR reversal.

SBA 3: B — Pelvic fracture = permissive hypotension + blood + pelvic binder/packing/angio.

SBA 4: B — Class II pulse 100–120.

SBA 5: B — Balanced crystalloids for dehydration (0.9% saline if severe hyponatraemia).

SBA 6: B — Lactate clearance ≥10–20% per 2h.

SBA 7: B — Base deficit >6 = significant hypoperfusion.

SBA 8: B — TXA 1g IV within 3h postpartum (WOMAN trial).

SBA 9: B — Flat neck veins = low preload = hypovolaemic.

SBA 10: B — 14–16G or IO access.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Icu Admission Criteria
topics.html:
Related: [[Critical Care Monitoring]], [[Early Warning Scores (NEWS2 / MEWS)]], [[End-of-Life Decisions in Acute Medicine]]

[!important]
ICU admission is not just for dying patients — early admission of the right patients saves lives. Use physiological criteria, not just diagnosis.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Know Level 1, 2, and 3 critical care definitions
  • Apply physiological admission criteria
  • Understand factors that may alter admission decisions
  • Communicate effectively with ICU team

2. Levels of Care (ICS / FICM)

LevelDescriptionMonitoring / Support
Level 0Ward careStandard monitoring
Level 1At risk / recently stepped downAdditional monitoring (e.g. NEWS2, NIV)
Level 2Single organ supportHDU / Level 2 bed (CPAP, vasoactive drugs, renal)
Level 3Multi-organ support / advanced respiratoryICU ventilator, ≥2 organ support

3. Absolute Indications for Level 2/3

  • Airway: Intubated, high-risk extubation, need for advanced airway management
  • Breathing: Invasive ventilation, NIV failure, severe hypoxaemia/hypercapnia
  • Circulation: Vasoactive infusions (noradrenaline, dobutamine, etc.), cardiac output monitoring
  • Neurology: GCS <8, sedation hold impossible, ICP monitoring, status epilepticus
  • Renal: Renal replacement therapy (CVVH, IHD)
  • Other: Complex multi-organ support, major surgery post-op

4. Relative / Soft Indications

  • NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained ≥5
  • High-risk surgery (planned post-op HDU/ICU)
  • Sepsis with organ dysfunction not responding to ward management
  • Severe electrolyte/metabolic disturbances needing close monitoring
  • High-risk transfers / repatriation

5. Factors Modifying Admission

  • Ceiling of care / DNACPR / patient wishes
  • Frailty (CFS >5) — may not benefit from invasive support
  • Prognosis — irreversible underlying disease
  • Resource availability — surge capacity

6. Red Flags for Deterioration (Call ICU Early)

  • Rising NEWS2
  • Increasing oxygen requirement
  • Falling GCS
  • New hypotension / lactate rise
  • Oliguria despite fluids

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • NEWS2 is a screening tool, not a mandate — clinical context matters
  • Level 2 = single organ support; Level 3 = multi-organ / invasive ventilation
  • Early discussion with ICU is always better than late referral
  • Document ceiling of care discussions clearly

8. Common Viva Questions

  1. What are the levels of critical care?
  2. When would you admit a septic patient to ICU vs HDU?
  3. How does frailty influence ICU admission decisions?

9. One-Page Revision Summary

Level 1: Ward + extra monitoring

Level 2: Single organ support (HDU)

Level 3: Multi-organ / invasive ventilation (ICU)

Admit for: vasoactive drugs, invasive vent, RRT, GCS <8

Use NEWS2 ≥7 as trigger, but clinical judgement > score

10. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Level 0–3 definitions (ICS/FICM)
  • Absolute indications: invasive vent, vasoactives, RRT, GCS <8
  • Level 2 = single organ; Level 3 = multi-organ/invasive vent
  • Early ICU discussion > late referral

Should Know

  • NEWS2 triggers (≥5 review, ≥7 urgent)
  • Ceiling of care / DNACPR / frailty (CFS) modify decisions
  • Soft indications: high-risk surgery, sepsis not responding

Nice to Know

  • Repatriation criteria
  • Surge capacity planning
  • Intermediate care / HDU step-down pathways

11. MCQs (10)

  1. Level 2 critical care (HDU) provides:

A. Ward care only

B. Single organ support

C. Multi-organ support

D. Invasive ventilation only

  1. Level 3 critical care (ICU) provides:

A. Ward care

B. Single organ support

C. Multi-organ support / invasive ventilation

D. Monitoring only

  1. Absolute indication for Level 3 care:

A. NEWS2 = 4

B. Invasive mechanical ventilation

C. Oxygen via nasal cannulae

D. Oral antibiotics

  1. Vasoactive drug infusion (noradrenaline) requires:

A. Level 0

B. Level 1

C. Level 2 (minimum)

D. Level 3 only

  1. Renal replacement therapy (CVVH) requires:

A. Level 1

B. Level 2

C. Level 3

D. Either Level 2 or 3

  1. GCS <8 with need for airway protection:

A. Level 1

B. Level 2

C. Level 3

D. Ward

  1. NEWS2 score ≥7 triggers:

A. Routine monitoring

B. Ward review

C. Urgent review / critical care outreach

D. DNACPR

  1. Frailty (CFS >5) influences ICU admission by:

A. Mandating admission

B. May not benefit from invasive support — guides ceiling of care

C. No influence

D. Automatic DNACPR

  1. Ceiling of care discussion should:

A. Be avoided

B. Be documented clearly

C. Be left to family only

D. Be done only at end of life

  1. Early ICU referral:

A. Worsens outcomes

B. Is always better than late referral

C. Increases mortality

D. Not necessary

12. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 68-year-old septic patient on ward, NEWS2 8, on 10L O₂, lactate 4.5, UO 0.2 mL/kg/hr. Best action:

A. Increase ward monitoring

B. Urgent ICU referral (NEWS2 ≥7 + organ dysfunction)

C. Wait for consultant ward round

D. Start oral antibiotics only

  1. A 75-year-old post-emergency laparotomy, on noradrenaline 0.1 µg/kg/min, extubated, UO adequate. Appropriate level:

A. Level 0

B. Level 1

C. Level 2 (vasoactive infusion = single organ support)

D. Level 3

  1. A 55-year-old with severe COPD exacerbation on NIV, pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 9.5, failing NIV. Next step:

A. Continue NIV

B. Intubate → Level 3 (invasive ventilation)

C. Increase O₂

D. Discharge

  1. An 85-year-old with CFS 7, metastatic cancer, develops sepsis. Family want "everything." ICU referral:

A. Admit to ICU immediately

B. Discuss ceiling of care — CFS 7 = severely frail, unlikely to benefit from invasive support

C. Ignore frailty

D. Admit to HDU only

  1. A patient on ward with NEWS2 5 sustained for 2 hours. Action:

A. Do nothing

B. Increase monitoring frequency, senior review

C. Call ICU immediately

D. Discharge

  1. Renal replacement therapy (CVVH) for AKI — level of care:

A. Level 1

B. Level 2

C. Level 3

D. Level 2 or 3 (organ support)

  1. A patient with GCS 7 after overdose, protecting airway, no other organ failure. Level:

A. Level 0

B. Level 1

C. Level 2 (neurological support)

D. Level 3

  1. High-risk elective surgery (e.g. oesophagectomy) — planned post-op:

A. Ward

B. Level 1

C. Level 2/3 (planned HDU/ICU)

D. Level 0

  1. A septic patient on noradrenaline 0.5 µg/kg/min, on NIV, lactate 3. Level:

A. Level 1

B. Level 2

C. Level 3 (≥2 organ support: cardiovascular + respiratory)

D. Ward

  1. Repatriation from ICU to referring hospital — criteria:

A. Stable, no Level 2/3 needs, accepting team available

B. Still on vasopressors

C. On invasive ventilation

D. Never repatriate

13. Flashcards

  • Q: Level 0/1/2/3

A: 0=ward, 1=at risk, 2=single organ (HDU), 3=multi-organ/invasive vent (ICU)

  • Q: Absolute ICU indications

A: Invasive vent, vasoactives, RRT, GCS<8, ICP monitor, multi-organ

  • Q: NEWS2 triggers

A: ≥5 review, ≥7 urgent/critical care outreach

  • Q: Frailty (CFS) and ICU

A: CFS >5 = may not benefit, guides ceiling of care

  • Q: Ceiling of care

A: Document clearly, discuss early

  • Q: Early ICU referral

A: Always better than late

14. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Level 2 = single organ support (HDU).

MCQ 2: C — Level 3 = multi-organ / invasive ventilation (ICU).

MCQ 3: B — Invasive ventilation = Level 3.

MCQ 4: C — Vasoactives = Level 2 minimum.

MCQ 5: C — RRT = organ support = Level 2/3.

MCQ 6: C — GCS <8 airway = Level 3.

MCQ 7: C — NEWS2 ≥7 = urgent review/critical care outreach.

MCQ 8: B — CFS >5 = severely frail, guides ceiling of care.

MCQ 9: B — Must be documented clearly.

MCQ 10: B — Early referral better than late.

SBA 1: B — NEWS2 8 + lactate + oliguria = ICU referral.

SBA 2: C — Vasoactive = single organ support = Level 2.

SBA 3: B — NIV failure = intubate = Level 3.

SBA 4: B — CFS 7 = severely frail, discuss ceiling of care.

SBA 5: B — NEWS2 5 sustained = increase frequency + senior review.

SBA 6: D — RRT = organ support = Level 2 or 3.

SBA 7: C — Neuro support = Level 2 if single organ (but GCS 7 often Level 3 for airway).

SBA 8: C — High-risk surgery = planned HDU/ICU.

SBA 9: C — Noradrenaline + NIV = 2 organ support = Level 3.

SBA 10: A — Stable, no organ support needs, accepting team.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Inotropes And Vasopressors
topics.html:
Related: [[Shock - Overview]], [[Critical Care Monitoring]], [[Fluid Resuscitation Strategies]]

[!important]
Vasopressors = increase SVR/BP. Inotropes = increase contractility/CO. Some drugs do both. Choose based on shock physiology.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate vasopressors, inotropes, and inodilators
  • Know receptor profiles and clinical effects
  • Choose first-line agent by shock type
  • Know dosing, titration, and complications

2. Drug Summary Table

DrugReceptorsPrimary EffectTypical DoseMain Use
Noradrenalineα₁ > β₁Vasoconstriction + mild inotropy0.01–0.5 µg/kg/minFirst-line septic / distributive
Adrenalineα₁ = β₁ = β₂Vasoconstriction + inotropy + chronotropy0.01–0.5 µg/kg/minSeptic shock refractory, anaphylaxis
Dobutamineβ₁ > β₂Inotropy + vasodilatation2.5–20 µg/kg/minCardiogenic shock (low output)
MilrinonePDE III inhibitorInotropy + vasodilatation0.25–0.75 µg/kg/minCardiogenic shock, weaning support
VasopressinV₁ receptorsVasoconstriction (non-catecholamine)0.01–0.04 units/minVasodilatory shock, catecholamine-sparing
Phenylephrineα₁ pureVasoconstriction only0.5–5 µg/kg/minNeurogenic shock, obstetrics

3. Choice by Shock Type

Shock TypeFirst-LineSecond-Line / Add-On
Septic / DistributiveNoradrenalineVasopressin (add), adrenaline
CardiogenicDobutamineNoradrenaline (if hypotensive), milrinone
ObstructiveNoradrenaline(Treat obstruction primarily)
AnaphylacticAdrenaline IMNoradrenaline IV if refractory
NeurogenicNoradrenalinePhenylephrine (if bradycardic)

4. Key Practical Points

  • Arterial line mandatory before starting (except adrenaline IM in anaphylaxis)
  • Central line preferred for noradrenaline/adrenaline (extravasation necrosis)
  • Dobutamine causes hypotension (vasodilatation) — often needs noradrenaline combo
  • Wean gradually when MAP stable, lactate normalising, urine output adequate

5. Red Flags

  • Peripheral extravasation → phentolamine infiltration
  • Tachyarrhythmias (especially with adrenaline/dobutamine)
  • limb ischaemia (vasoconstriction)

6. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Noradrenaline = first-line in septic shock
  • Dobutamine = first-line in cardiogenic shock (add noradrenaline if hypotensive)
  • Vasopressin spares catecholamines
  • Milrinone = avoid in hypotension (pure vasodilator + inotrope)

7. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the first-line vasopressor in septic shock?
  2. Why do you sometimes need to combine noradrenaline with dobutamine?
  3. What is the dose of vasopressin in vasodilatory shock?

8. One-Page Revision Summary

Noradrenaline = septic/distributive (first-line)

Dobutamine = cardiogenic (first-line)

Adrenaline = anaphylaxis, refractory septic

Vasopressin = catecholamine-sparing add-on

Milrinone = cardiogenic (if noradrenaline/dobutamine inadequate)

9. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Noradrenaline first-line septic/distributive
  • Dobutamine first-line cardiogenic
  • Adrenaline for anaphylaxis/refractory septic
  • Vasopressin add-on (catecholamine-sparing)
  • Arterial line mandatory (except adrenaline IM anaphylaxis)
  • Central line preferred for noradrenaline/adrenaline

Should Know

  • Dobutamine causes hypotension (vasodilatation) → often needs noradrenaline combo
  • Milrinone = avoid in hypotension (pure vasodilator + inotrope)
  • Phenylephrine for neurogenic shock (if bradycardic)
  • Weaning criteria: MAP stable, lactate normalising, UO adequate

Nice to Know

  • Angiotensin II for refractory vasoplegia
  • Levosimendan (Ca²⁺ sensitiser) alternative in cardiogenic
  • Dopamine — no longer recommended (arrhythmia risk)
  • Fenoldopam, nitroglycerin, nitroprusside for afterload reduction

10. MCQs (10)

  1. First-line vasopressor in septic shock:

A. Adrenaline

B. Noradrenaline

C. Vasopressin

D. Dopamine

  1. First-line inotrope in cardiogenic shock:

A. Noradrenaline

B. Dobutamine

C. Milrinone

D. Adrenaline

  1. Noradrenaline receptor profile:

A. α₁ = β₁ = β₂

B. α₁ > β₁

C. β₁ > α₁

D. Pure α₁

  1. Dobutamine causes:

A. Vasoconstriction

B. Vasodilatation (β₂) + inotropy (β₁)

C. Pure inotropy

D. Pure vasoconstriction

  1. Vasopressin dose in septic shock:

A. 0.1–0.4 U/min

B. 0.01–0.04 U/min

C. 1–4 U/min

D. 10–40 U/min

  1. Adrenaline in anaphylaxis — route and dose:

A. IV 1mg

B. IM 0.5mg (anterolateral thigh)

C. SC 0.5mg

D. IO 1mg

  1. Vasopressin is added to noradrenaline when:

A. Immediately

B. Noradrenaline ~0.25–0.5 µg/kg/min (catecholamine-sparing)

C. Only if noradrenaline >2 µg/kg/min

D. Never

  1. Milrinone is:

A. β₁ agonist

B. PDE III inhibitor (inodilator)

C. α₁ agonist

D. V₁ agonist

  1. Monitoring mandatory before starting noradrenaline infusion:

A. CVP

B. Arterial line

C. Cardiac output

D. ICP

  1. Phenylephrine preferred in:

A. Septic shock

B. Cardiogenic shock

C. Neurogenic shock (if bradycardic)

D. Anaphylaxis

11. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A septic patient on noradrenaline 0.5 µg/kg/min, MAP 60, lactate 4. Best addition:

A. Increase noradrenaline to 2 µg/kg/min

B. Add vasopressin 0.03 U/min

C. Switch to adrenaline

D. Add dobutamine

  1. A cardiogenic shock patient (LVEF 25%, pulmonary oedema), BP 85/50, on dobutamine 10 µg/kg/min. Still hypotensive. Best addition:

A. Increase dobutamine

B. Add noradrenaline

C. Add milrinone

D. Add vasopressin

  1. Anaphylactic shock — first and most important drug:

A. IV hydrocortisone

B. IM chlorphenamine

C. IM adrenaline 0.5mg

D. IV fluids

  1. A patient on noradrenaline 1.5 µg/kg/min + vasopressin 0.04 U/min, MAP 55, lactate rising. Echo shows hyperdynamic. Next:

A. Add adrenaline

B. Add dobutamine

C. Add milrinone

D. Increase vasopressin

  1. Dobutamine in cardiogenic shock — common side effect requiring combo:

A. Bradycardia

B. Hypotension (vasodilatation)

C. Hypertension

D. Tachycardia only

  1. Milrinone contraindicated in:

A. Hypertension

B. Hypotension (pure vasodilator)

C. Bradycardia

D. Tachycardia

  1. Noradrenaline extravasation treatment:

A. Elevate limb

B. Phentolamine infiltration

C. Ice pack

D. Stop infusion only

  1. Weaning vasopressors — criteria:

A. MAP >65 only

B. MAP stable + lactate normalising + UO adequate

C. Lactate normal only

D. UO adequate only

  1. Angiotensin II indicated for:

A. First-line septic shock

B. Refractory vasodilatory shock despite catecholamines + vasopressin

C. Cardiogenic shock

D. Anaphylaxis

  1. Dopamine in shock — current recommendation:

A. First-line

B. Second-line

C. Not recommended (arrhythmia risk)

D. Only in cardiogenic

12. Flashcards

  • Q: Noradrenaline =

A: First-line septic/distributive (α₁ > β₁)

  • Q: Dobutamine =

A: First-line cardiogenic (β₁ > β₂), causes vasodilatation

  • Q: Adrenaline =

A: Anaphylaxis (IM 0.5mg), refractory septic (IV)

  • Q: Vasopressin =

A: Catecholamine-sparing add-on (V₁ receptor), 0.01–0.04 U/min

  • Q: Milrinone =

A: PDE III inhibitor, inodilator, avoid in hypotension

  • Q: Phenylephrine =

A: Neurogenic shock (pure α₁, no tachycardia)

  • Q: Arterial line mandatory for

A: Noradrenaline, adrenaline IV, vasopressin

  • Q: Dobutamine hypotension

A: Add noradrenaline

  • Q: Weaning criteria

A: MAP stable + lactate normalising + UO adequate

13. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Noradrenaline first-line septic.

MCQ 2: B — Dobutamine first-line cardiogenic.

MCQ 3: B — α₁ > β₁.

MCQ 4: B — β₁ inotropy + β₂ vasodilatation.

MCQ 5: B — 0.01–0.04 U/min.

MCQ 6: B — IM 0.5mg anterolateral thigh.

MCQ 7: B — At NA ~0.25–0.5 as catecholamine-sparing.

MCQ 8: B — PDE III inhibitor.

MCQ 9: B — Arterial line mandatory.

MCQ 10: C — Pure α₁, no β effect → no tachycardia.

SBA 1: B — Add vasopressin at NA 0.5 (catecholamine-sparing).

SBA 2: B — Cardiogenic + hypotension on dobutamine → add noradrenaline (vasopressor for perfusion pressure).

SBA 3: C — IM adrenaline 0.5mg first-line.

SBA 4: A — Refractory hyperdynamic septic shock → add adrenaline (β₁ inotropy + α vasoconstriction).

SBA 5: B — Dobutamine β₂ vasodilatation → hypotension.

SBA 6: B — Milrinone = vasodilator + inotrope, worsens hypotension.

SBA 7: B — Phentolamine infiltration for extravasation.

SBA 8: B — All three criteria for weaning.

SBA 9: B — Refractory vasodilatory shock salvage.

SBA 10: C — Dopamine not recommended (SOAP-II trial: more arrhythmias).

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Invasive Mechanical Ventilation Basics
topics.html:
Related: [[Acute Respiratory Failure]], [[Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV)]]

[!important]
Invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) = positive-pressure ventilation via an endotracheal tube or tracheostomy. Used when NIV fails or is contraindicated.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Know indications for intubation and IMV
  • Understand basic ventilator modes and settings
  • Recognise common complications
  • Apply lung-protective ventilation principles

2. Indications for Intubation / IMV

  • Apnoea / respiratory arrest
  • GCS ≤8 (airway protection)
  • Refractory hypoxaemia (SpO₂ <90% on high-flow O₂)
  • Refractory hypercapnia / acidosis (pH <7.25)
  • Impending fatigue (rising RR, accessory muscle use)
  • Severe shock with high ventilatory demand

3. Basic Modes

ModeDescriptionUse
VCV (Volume Control)Set tidal volume, RRStandard mode, guarantees minute volume
PCV (Pressure Control)Set inspiratory pressureBetter pressure limitation, ARDS
PRVC / SIMVPressure-regulated volume / Synchronised IMVWeaning, transitional

4. Lung-Protective Ventilation (ARDSNet)

  • Tidal volume: 6 mL/kg predicted body weight
  • Plateau pressure: <30 cmH₂O
  • PEEP: titrated to FiO₂ (ARDSNet table)
  • FiO₂: target SpO₂ 88–95% (PaO₂ 8–12 kPa)
  • Permissive hypercapnia accepted (pH >7.20)

5. Key Settings

  • RR: 12–20 (adjust for pH/CO₂)
  • PEEP: 5–15 cmH₂O (higher in ARDS)
  • FiO₂: lowest to maintain target SpO₂
  • I:E ratio: 1:2 normally; inverse in severe ARDS

6. Complications

  • Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP)
  • Barotrauma / volutrauma
  • Haemodynamic compromise (reduced venous return)
  • Ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI)

7. Red Flags

  • Rising peak / plateau pressures
  • Sudden hypotension post-intubation
  • Auto-PEEP (dynamic hyperinflation)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg) is the single most evidence-based intervention in ARDS
  • Always check for auto-PEEP in obstructive airways disease
  • Hypotension after intubation = usually hypovolaemia or reduced preload

9. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is lung-protective ventilation?
  2. What tidal volume do you use in ARDS?
  3. How do you manage auto-PEEP?

10. One-Page Revision Summary

IMV = intubation + positive pressure

Lung-protective: 6 mL/kg, Pplat <30, PEEP titrated

VCV or PCV acceptable

Complications: VAP, barotrauma, hypotension

11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Indications for intubation (GCS ≤8, refractory hypoxaemia/hypercapnia, fatigue)
  • VCV vs PCV differences
  • ARDSNet lung-protective: 6 mL/kg PBW, Pplat <30, PEEP/FiO₂ table
  • Permissive hypercapnia (pH >7.20)

Should Know

  • Auto-PEEP recognition and management
  • VAP prevention bundle
  • Weaning modes (SIMV, PS, CPAP trials)

Nice to Know

  • Advanced modes (APRV, HFOV, ECMO)
  • Neuromuscular blockade in severe ARDS
  • Prone positioning criteria

12. MCQs (10)

  1. Tidal volume in ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation:

A. 8 mL/kg actual body weight

B. 6 mL/kg predicted body weight

C. 10 mL/kg ideal body weight

D. 12 mL/kg predicted body weight

  1. Target plateau pressure in ARDS:

A. <25 cmH₂O

B. <30 cmH₂O

C. <35 cmH₂O

D. <40 cmH₂O

  1. VCV vs PCV — which guarantees minute volume?

A. VCV

B. PCV

C. Both

D. Neither

  1. Permissive hypercapnia pH limit:

A. >7.10

B. >7.20

C. >7.30

D. >7.40

  1. Auto-PEEP is most common in:

A. ARDS

B. Obstructive airways disease (COPD/asthma)

C. Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema

D. Neuromuscular disease

  1. Management of auto-PEEP:

A. Increase PEEP

B. Increase tidal volume

C. Decrease RR, increase I:E ratio, disconnect circuit briefly

D. Increase FiO₂

  1. VAP prevention bundle includes all EXCEPT:

A. HOB elevation 30–45°

B. Daily sedation hold + SBT

C. Routine antibiotic prophylaxis

D. Subglottic suctioning

  1. Hypotension immediately post-intubation most commonly due to:

A. Anaphylaxis to induction drugs

B. Hypovolaemia / reduced venous return

C. Tension pneumothorax

D. Myocardial infarction

  1. PEEP in ARDS is titrated to:

A. FiO₂ (ARDSNet table)

B. Plateau pressure

C. Tidal volume

D. RR

  1. Mode best for weaning:

A. VCV

B. PCV

C. SIMV / PS / CPAP

D. Inverse ratio

13. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 55-year-old man with severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ 80) on VCV. Current settings: TV 500 mL (PBW 70 kg), PEEP 10, FiO₂ 1.0, RR 24. Plateau pressure 38. Best adjustment:

A. Increase PEEP to 15

B. Reduce tidal volume to 420 mL (6 mL/kg PBW)

C. Increase FiO₂ (already 1.0)

D. Switch to PCV only

  1. A 68-year-old COPD patient intubated for exacerbation. Suddenly hypotensive, high peak pressures, difficult to ventilate. Most likely:

A. Tension pneumothorax

B. Auto-PEEP

C. Endobronchial intubation

D. Pulmonary embolism

  1. A patient on VCV with TV 6 mL/kg PBW, PEEP 12, FiO₂ 0.8. PaO₂ 8 kPa. Next step:

A. Increase TV to 8 mL/kg

B. Increase PEEP per ARDSNet table

C. Decrease PEEP

D. Switch to PCV

  1. VAP diagnosis requires:

A. New infiltrate + purulent sputum + fever

B. Positive blood cultures only

C. Fever only

D. New infiltrate only

  1. A 40-year-old asthmatic intubated. Rising peak pressures, hypotension. Immediate action:

A. Increase RR

B. Disconnect circuit, allow exhalation, reduce RR, increase I:E

C. Give adrenaline

D. Increase PEEP

  1. Daily sedation hold + SBT is part of:

A. VAP prevention bundle

B. ARDSNet protocol

C. Lung-protective ventilation

D. Prone positioning

  1. Neuromuscular blockade in ARDS (ACURASYS/PROSEVA):

A. Routine for all ARDS

B. 48-hour infusion in severe ARDS (P/F <150)

C. Only if ventilator dyssynchrony

D. Contraindicated

  1. Prone positioning in severe ARDS:

A. 4 hours/day

B. 8 hours/day

C. 12–16 hours/day

D. 24 hours/day

  1. Pressure support ventilation (PSV) is used for:

A. Full support

B. Weaning / spontaneous breathing trials

C. ARDS only

D. Never

  1. Peak pressure vs plateau pressure difference reflects:

A. Alveolar pressure

B. Airway resistance

C. Lung compliance

D. Chest wall compliance

14. Flashcards

  • Q: ARDSNet tidal volume

A: 6 mL/kg predicted body weight

  • Q: Plateau pressure target

A: <30 cmH₂O

  • Q: Permissive hypercapnia pH

A: >7.20

  • Q: Auto-PEEP management

A: Disconnect circuit, reduce RR, increase I:E, increase expiratory time

  • Q: VAP bundle

A: HOB 30–45°, daily sedation hold/SBT, subglottic suction, peptic ulcer prophylaxis, DVT prophylaxis

  • Q: Post-intubation hypotension

A: Usually hypovolaemia / reduced venous return

  • Q: PEEP titration in ARDS

A: ARDSNet PEEP/FiO₂ table

15. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — 6 mL/kg PBW (not actual weight).

MCQ 2: B — <30 cmH₂O.

MCQ 3: A — VCV guarantees set minute volume.

MCQ 4: B — pH >7.20 accepted.

MCQ 5: B — COPD/asthma (expiratory flow limitation).

MCQ 6: C — Reduce RR, increase I:E, allow full exhalation.

MCQ 7: C — Routine antibiotic prophylaxis NOT in VAP bundle.

MCQ 8: B — Reduced venous return from +ve pressure ventilation.

MCQ 9: A — ARDSNet PEEP/FiO₂ table.

MCQ 10: C — SIMV/PS/CPAP for weaning.

SBA 1: B — Pplat 38 >30 → reduce TV to 6 mL/kg (420 mL for 70 kg PBW).

SBA 2: B — COPD + hypotension + high pressures = auto-PEEP; disconnect, reduce RR.

SBA 3: B — P/F 100 (8/0.8) = moderate ARDS; increase PEEP per table.

SBA 4: A — New infiltrate + purulent sputum + fever/leukocytosis.

SBA 5: B — Auto-PEEP in asthma: disconnect, reduce RR, prolong expiration.

SBA 6: A — VAP bundle includes daily sedation hold + SBT.

SBA 7: B — 48h NMB in severe ARDS (P/F <150) per ACURASYS.

SBA 8: C — 12–16 hours/day per PROSEVA.

SBA 9: B — PSV for weaning/SBT.

SBA 10: B — Peak – plateau = airway resistance.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Major Haemorrhage And Massive Transfusion
topics.html:
Related: [[Hypovolaemic Shock]], [[Acid-Base and Electrolyte Emergencies]]

[!important]
Major haemorrhage = life-threatening bleed; MTP activation when ≥4-10 units RBC in 24 h, ≥4 units in 1 h, or anticipated need (trauma, ruptured AAA, postpartum). 1:1:1 ratio (RBC:FFP:Platelets) for damage control resuscitation. TXA 1 g IV within 3 h of injury (CRASH-2). Fibrinogen 2-4 g if Clauss fibrinogen <1.5 g/L (target >1.5-2.0). Calcium gluconate 10 mL per 4 units RBC (citrate toxicity → hypocalcaemia). Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) until surgical control, except TBI (MAP ≥80). Warm patient, warm fluids, avoid acidosis + coagulopathy + hypothermia = "lethal triad". FCPS/MRCP: MTP criteria, 1:1:1, TXA timing, calcium, fibrinogen threshold, viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG), reversal of anticoagulants (vitamin K, PCC, idarucizumab, andexanet).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define major haemorrhage and MTP activation criteria
  • Apply damage control resuscitation (1:1:1, permissive hypotension, TXA)
  • Recognise and treat coagulopathy (hypothermia, acidosis, hypocalcaemia)
  • Reverse anticoagulants in major bleeding
  • Use viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG) for targeted transfusion
  • Identify and treat hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, TRALI, TACO
  • Manage obstetric haemorrhage specifically

2. Definitions

  • Major haemorrhage (BCSH): bleed requiring ≥4 units RBC in <1 h OR ongoing bleeding + SBP <90 + HR >110 + base deficit >-6
  • Massive transfusion: ≥10 units RBC in 24 h OR ≥4 units in 1 h
  • Damage control resuscitation (DCR): balanced blood product transfusion to mimic whole blood, permissive hypotension, avoid crystalloid
  • Lethal triad: hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy

3. Aetiology

CategoryExamples
TraumaRoad traffic accident, falls, penetrating injury
SurgicalVascular rupture, intra-op bleed
GIVariceal bleed, peptic ulcer, diverticular, aortoenteric fistula
ObstetricPostpartum haemorrhage (PPH), ectopic, abruption
VascularRuptured AAA, aortic dissection
HaematologicalCoagulopathy, DIC, ITP
Anticoagulant-relatedWarfarin, DOAC overdose

4. Initial Assessment — ABCDE

  1. Airway + C-spine protection
  2. Breathing: high-flow O₂, treat tension PTX
  3. Circulation: 2 large-bore IVs (14-16G) or central access; bloods including crossmatch, FBC, coagulation, fibrinogen, U&E, lactate, ABG, calcium
  4. Disability: GCS, pupils
  5. Exposure: full exam, log roll, warm patient

5. Investigations

  • FBC: Hb may be normal initially (not diluted yet)
  • Coagulation: PT, aPTT, INR
  • Fibrinogen (Clauss): target >1.5 g/L (obstetric >2.0)
  • U&E: K⁺ (hyperkalaemia from stored blood)
  • ABG/lactate: tissue hypoperfusion marker
  • Ionised calcium: citrate in stored blood binds Ca²⁺ → hypocalcaemia
  • Viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG): rapid, point-of-care; guides targeted transfusion
  • Group and save / crossmatch (uncrossmatched O-negative if critical)

6. Massive Transfusion Protocol (MTP)

Activation Criteria

  • ≥4 units RBC in <1 h
  • ≥10 units RBC anticipated in 24 h
  • SBP <90 + HR >110 + base deficit >-6
  • Activated by senior clinician (ED, anaesthetics, surgery)

MTP Bundle (Damage Control Resuscitation)

ComponentInitial Action
RBCGroup O-neg until crossmatched; switch to type-specific
FFP1:1 ratio with RBC (15 mL/kg)
Platelets1:1:1 ratio; aim >50 × 10⁹/L (>100 in TBI/eye/CNS)
Cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen2 pools (10 units) if fibrinogen <1.5 g/L (target >1.5)
TXA1 g IV within 3 h of injury, then 1 g over 8 h
Calcium10% Ca gluconate 10 mL per 4 units RBC (or 1 g CaCl₂)
Warm fluidsBair Hugger, fluid warmers, ambient warming
Permissive hypotensionSBP 80-90 (MAP 50-60) until surgical control (EXCEPT TBI)

1:1:1 Ratio (PROPPR Trial)

  • RBC : FFP : Platelets = 1:1:1
  • Equivalent to whole blood
  • Reduced death from exsanguination at 24 h vs 1:1:2
  • Reduced death from haemorrhage

7. Specific Therapies

TXA (Tranexamic Acid)

  • Indication: major trauma bleeding within 3 h of injury
  • Dose: 1 g IV over 10 min, then 1 g over 8 h
  • Mechanism: antifibrinolytic, blocks plasminogen activation
  • CRASH-2 trial: reduced all-cause mortality (14.5% vs 16.0%) when given <3 h
  • Caution: increased mortality if given >3 h
  • WOMAN trial (PPH): 1 g IV; reduced death from bleeding
  • Contraindications: active thromboembolic disease, subarachnoid haemorrhage

Calcium

  • Why: citrate in stored blood → binds Ca²⁺ → hypocalcaemia
  • Dose: 10% Ca gluconate 10 mL per 4 units RBC (or 1 g CaCl₂ central line)
  • Target: ionised Ca²⁺ >1.1 mmol/L
  • Important: hypocalcaemia worsens coagulopathy + cardiac depression

Fibrinogen

  • Threshold: <1.5 g/L (obstetric <2.0 g/L) → give cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate
  • Dose: 2 pools cryoprecipitate (10 units, ~4 g fibrinogen) OR 2-4 g fibrinogen concentrate
  • Source: cryo (FFP precipitate) or concentrate (Riastap)

Platelets

  • Threshold: <50 × 10⁹/L (or <100 in TBI/eye)
  • Dose: 1 adult dose (typically 1 pool of 5-6 donations or apheresis)
  • Continuous infusion in some MTP protocols

8. Reversal of Anticoagulants in Major Bleeding

AnticoagulantReversal AgentDose
WarfarinVitamin K + PCC (4-factor)Vit K 5-10 mg IV + PCC 25-50 U/kg
DabigatranIdarucizumab5 g IV (2 × 2.5 g)
ApixabanAndexanet alfa (or PCC 4-factor 50 U/kg)Andexanet bolus + infusion; PCC alternative
RivaroxabanAndexanet alfa (or PCC 4-factor 50 U/kg)Andexanet bolus + infusion; PCC alternative
Heparin (UFH)Protamine1 mg per 100 U heparin (last 2-3 h)
LMWH (enoxaparin)Protamine (partial)1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin; 50% reversal
Aspirin/clopidogrelPlatelet transfusion (consider)1 adult dose
TPA/streptokinaseCryoprecipitate, FFP, TXASupportive

9. Permissive Hypotension

  • Target SBP 80-90 mmHg until bleeding controlled
  • Mechanism: avoid "pop the clot" by elevating BP
  • EXCEPTIONS:
  • Traumatic brain injury: SBP ≥110 (or MAP ≥80)
  • Spinal cord injury: MAP ≥85
  • Pregnancy: SBP 100-110 (uterine perfusion)
  • Penetrating torso: shorter time to OR → less fluid

10. Viscoelastic Testing (ROTEM/TEG)

  • Point-of-care tests of whole blood clot formation
  • ROTEM: EXTEM (extrinsic), INTEM (intrinsic), FIBTEM (fibrinogen), HEPTEM (heparin effect)
  • TEG: kaolin, rapid TEG, functional fibrinogen
  • Advantages over standard coagulation: faster, functional, identifies hyperfibrinolysis
  • Guides targeted transfusion (e.g., FIBTEM MCF <7 mm → give fibrinogen)

11. Obstetric Haemorrhage (PPH)

  • Definition: >500 mL (vaginal) or >1000 mL (CS) blood loss, or any bleed causing haemodynamic compromise
  • Primary (24 h) vs secondary (24 h-6 weeks)
  • Causes: 4 T's — Tone (atony, 70%), Trauma (lacerations), Tissue (retained), Thrombin (coagulopathy)
  • Management:
  1. 2 large-bore IVs, crossmatch 4-6 units
  2. Oxytocin 5 U IV + 40 U in 500 mL over 4 h
  3. Ergometrine 0.2 mg IV/IM (avoid in HTN)
  4. Carboprost (Hemabate) 250 mcg IM q15-90 min (avoid in asthma)
  5. Misoprostol 800 mcg PR if other uterotonics fail
  6. TXA 1 g IV (WOMAN trial)
  7. Bimanual compression, intrauterine balloon (Bakri), B-Lynch suture
  8. Surgical: uterine artery ligation, B-Lynch suture, hysterectomy
  9. Activate MTP

12. Complications of Massive Transfusion

ComplicationCauseManagement
HypothermiaCold productsWarm fluids, blanket, ambient
HypocalcaemiaCitrate toxicity10 mL Ca gluconate per 4 units RBC
HyperkalaemiaStored blood lysisInsulin/dextrose, Ca²⁺, dialysis
AcidosisTissue hypoperfusion + stored bloodTreat underlying, consider NaHCO₃
CoagulopathyDilutional, consumptionFFP, platelets, cryo
TRALIDonor antibodiesSupportive, O₂, ventilation
TACOVolume overloadDiuretics, slow transfusion
DICUnderlying shockTreat cause, blood products
ARDSSIRS, transfusionsSupportive ventilation

13. Special Situations

Ruptured AAA

  • Permissive hypotension + activate MTP
  • Endovascular (EVAR) vs open repair
  • Allowed SBP 80-100

Variceal Bleeding

  • See [[Acute Medicine in Pregnancy]]/GI bleeding chapter
  • Resuscitate to Hb 70-80 (restrictive)
  • Terlipressin 2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg q4h
  • Antibiotics (ceftriaxone)
  • Endoscopic band ligation

Anticoagulant-Related Bleeding

  • See table above for reversal

14. Prognosis

  • Trauma haemorrhage mortality: 30-50% if uncontrolled
  • MTP with 1:1:1: 24 h mortality 17% (PROPPR)
  • Trauma deaths: 30% pre-hospital, 30% intra-op, 30% post-op (coagulopathy, SIRS)

15. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  1. Major haemorrhage: ≥4 units RBC in <1 h OR ongoing bleeding + SBP <90 + HR >110
  2. MTP activation: senior clinician
  3. Damage control resuscitation (DCR): 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets) + permissive hypotension + avoid crystalloid
  4. TXA 1 g IV within 3 h of injury (CRASH-2)
  5. Fibrinogen target >1.5 g/L (obstetric >2.0)
  6. Calcium 10 mL 10% per 4 units RBC
  7. Lethal triad: hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy
  8. Permissive hypotension SBP 80-90 (EXCEPT TBI MAP ≥80, pregnancy SBP 100-110)
  9. Group O-neg until crossmatched
  10. Viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG) for targeted transfusion
  11. Warfarin reversal: Vitamin K 5-10 mg IV + PCC 4-factor
  12. Dabigatran reversal: Idarucizumab 5 g
  13. Apixaban/rivaroxaban reversal: Andexanet alfa (or PCC)
  14. Heparin reversal: Protamine 1 mg/100 U
  15. PPH 4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin
  16. CRASH-2: TXA within 3 h
  17. WOMAN trial: TXA 1 g IV in PPH

16. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define massive transfusion
  2. List MTP activation criteria
  3. Damage control resuscitation components
  4. TXA indications, dose, timing
  5. Reversal of warfarin, dabigatran, apixaban
  6. Fibrinogen threshold and replacement
  7. Permissive hypotension indications
  8. ROTEM/TEG interpretation
  9. PPH 4 T's and management
  10. Complications of massive transfusion

17. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Hb may be normal initially in acute bleed (not yet haemodiluted)
  • TXA only within 3 h (mortality ↑ if later)
  • Calcium is essential (citrate toxicity)
  • Permissive hypotension EXCEPT in TBI (MAP ≥80)
  • FFP and platelets 1:1:1 with RBC (not 1:1:2)
  • Cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen (FFP alone won't raise fibrinogen adequately)
  • Fibrinogen target higher in obstetric (>2.0)
  • Vitamin K + PCC for warfarin (not vitamin K alone if active bleed)
  • Andexanet for DOAC reversal (factor Xa inhibitors)
  • Idarucizumab for dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor)
  • TRALI vs TACO: TRALI = acute lung injury; TACO = fluid overload
  • Stored blood has no platelets/function → need platelet transfusion

18. Mnemonics

  • MTP criteria: 4 units in 1 h, OR 10 in 24 h
  • DCR components: 1:1:1, TXA, Calcium, Warm, Permissive hypotension
  • TXA: 1 g IV within 3 h, then 1 g over 8 h
  • Calcium: 10 mL per 4 units RBC
  • Fibrinogen: >1.5 (>2.0 obstetric)
  • Lethal triad: HAC (Hypothermia, Acidosis, Coagulopathy)
  • PPH 4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin
  • Warfarin reversal: Vitamin K + PCC
  • Dabigatran: Idarucizumab
  • Apixaban/Rivaroxaban: Andexanet (or PCC)
  • Permissive hypotension: SBP 80-90 (TBI excluded)

19. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Major Haemorrhage / MTP))

Definitions

Major haemorrhage: 4 units/h or SBP<90+HR>110

MTP: 10 units in 24h

Aetiology

Trauma, surgical, GI, obstetric, vascular

MTP Bundle

1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

TXA 1g IV <3h

Calcium 10mL/4 units

Fibrinogen >1.5

Warm patient

Permissive hypotension

Drugs

TXA (CRASH-2)

Calcium gluconate

Fibrinogen / cryo

Vitamin K, PCC

Idarucizumab (dabigatran)

Andexanet (apixaban/riva)

Reversal

Warfarin: Vit K + PCC

DOAC: Idarucizumab/Andexanet

Heparin: Protamine

LMWH: Protamine partial

Viscoelastic

ROTEM

TEG

Targets: FIBTEM MCF >7mm

Permissive Hypotension

SBP 80-90

Except TBI MAP >=80

Pregnancy SBP 100-110

Complications

Lethal triad

Hypocalcaemia

Hyperkalaemia

TRALI, TACO

DIC, ARDS

PPH

4 Ts Tone Trauma Tissue Thrombin

Oxytocin, ergometrine, carboprost, misoprostol

TXA

Surgical: B-Lynch, hysterectomy

```

20. Flowchart — MTP Activation

```mermaid

flowchart TD

A[Major Haemorrhage] --> B[MTP Activated by Senior]

B --> C[2x Large-bore IVs, Group O-neg until cross]

C --> D[MTP Pack 1: 4 RBC + 4 FFP + 1 Platelets]

D --> E[Repeat Pack]

E --> F[Calcium 10mL/4 units]

F --> G[TXA 1g IV within 3h]

G --> H[Viscoelastic testing: ROTEM/TEG]

H --> I[Fibrinogen <1.5: Cryoprecipitate 2 pools]

I --> J[Anticoagulant reversal if needed]

J --> K{Surgical Control?}

K -->|No| L[Continue MTP, surgical/radiology]

K -->|Yes| M[Stop MTP, monitor]

```

21. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Major haemorrhage: ≥4 units/h, SBP <90, HR >110, ongoing bleeding
  • MTP = 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets) = damage control resuscitation
  • TXA 1 g IV within 3 h (CRASH-2)
  • Calcium 10 mL 10% per 4 units RBC
  • Fibrinogen target >1.5 g/L (>2.0 obstetric)
  • Permissive hypotension SBP 80-90 (TBI exception: MAP ≥80)
  • Warm patient (lethal triad: hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy)
  • Warfarin: Vitamin K + PCC; Dabigatran: Idarucizumab; Apixaban/Riva: Andexanet (or PCC)
  • Viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG) for targeted transfusion
  • PPH 4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • State MTP activation criteria
  • Describe damage control resuscitation
  • List reversal of warfarin, dabigatran, apixaban
  • State TXA dose and timing
  • Outline PPH 4 T's and management

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

22. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Major haemorrhage definition
  • MTP activation criteria
  • 1:1:1 ratio (RBC:FFP:Platelets)
  • TXA 1 g within 3 h
  • Calcium 10 mL per 4 units RBC
  • Fibrinogen target >1.5
  • Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90)
  • Lethal triad (HAC)
  • PPH 4 T's

Should Know

  • Reversal of warfarin, dabigatran, apixaban
  • ROTEM/TEG interpretation
  • FIBTEM MCF target
  • LMWH reversal (protamine partial)
  • TRALI vs TACO
  • PPH pharmacological ladder
  • Permissive hypotension exclusions (TBI)
  • PPH surgical options (B-Lynch, hysterectomy)
  • Stored blood electrolyte issues
  • Citrate toxicity

Nice to Know

  • PROPPR trial details
  • CRASH-2 mortality numbers
  • Andexanet dosing details
  • Viscoelastic test parameters
  • Cryoprecipitate composition
  • EVAR in ruptured AAA
  • rFVIIa (off-label)
  • Fibrinogen concentrate (Riastap)
  • Viscoelastic algorithm details

23. Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

24. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • Definition major haemorrhage + MTP
  • Aetiology (trauma, surgical, GI, obstetric, vascular, haematological)
  • Initial assessment (ABCDE)
  • Investigations (FBC, coag, fibrinogen, ABG, ionised Ca, ROTEM/TEG)
  • MTP bundle (1:1:1, TXA, calcium, warm, permissive hypotension)
  • Reversal of anticoagulants
  • PPH 4 T's and management
  • Complications (TRALI, TACO, hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, ARDS)
  • Viscoelastic testing

Short Note Skeleton

  • MTP activation criteria
  • Damage control resuscitation
  • TXA dose and timing
  • Reversal of warfarin, DOACs
  • PPH management

Viva One-Liners

  • "MTP: ≥4 units/h or 10 units in 24 h"
  • "DCR: 1:1:1, TXA, calcium, warm, permissive hypotension"
  • "TXA 1 g IV within 3 h (CRASH-2)"
  • "Calcium 10 mL per 4 units RBC"
  • "Fibrinogen target >1.5 g/L"
  • "Permissive hypotension SBP 80-90 (TBI excluded)"
  • "Lethal triad: hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy"
  • "Warfarin reversal: Vit K + PCC"
  • "Dabigatran reversal: Idarucizumab"
  • "Apixaban/rivaroxaban reversal: Andexanet (or PCC)"
  • "PPH 4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Trauma MTP: 1:1:1 + TXA + calcium + warm + permissive hypotension
  • Warfarin + GI bleed: Vit K + PCC + endoscopy
  • DOAC + intracranial bleed: PCC or specific antidote (Andexanet/Idarucizumab)
  • PPH: 4 T's, uterotonics, TXA, balloon, surgery if needed
  • Ruptured AAA: permissive hypotension, MTP, EVAR vs open

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • MTP criteria: 4 units/h
  • 1:1:1 ratio
  • TXA 1 g IV <3 h
  • Calcium 10 mL/4 units
  • Fibrinogen >1.5
  • Permissive SBP 80-90 (TBI excluded)
  • HAC = lethal triad
  • Warfarin → Vit K + PCC
  • Dabigatran → Idarucizumab
  • Apixaban/Riva → Andexanet
  • PPH 4 T's

25. Summary

Major haemorrhage = life-threatening bleed requiring rapid, balanced transfusion. MTP activation criteria: ≥4 units RBC in <1 h, ≥10 units in 24 h, or anticipated need (SBP <90, HR >110, base deficit >-6). Damage Control Resuscitation (DCR): 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets) (PROPPR), permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90), avoid crystalloid, TXA 1 g IV within 3 h (CRASH-2), then 1 g over 8 h. Calcium 10 mL 10% per 4 units RBC (citrate toxicity). Fibrinogen target >1.5 g/L (obstetric >2.0) — give cryoprecipitate 2 pools. Lethal triad: Hypothermia + Acidosis + Coagulopathy. Permissive hypotension EXCEPTIONS: TBI (MAP ≥80), pregnancy (SBP 100-110), spinal cord injury. Viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG) for targeted transfusion (FIBTEM MCF >7 mm). Anticoagulant reversal: Warfarin → Vit K 5-10 mg + PCC 25-50 U/kg; Dabigatran → Idarucizumab 5 g; Apixaban/Rivaroxaban → Andexanet (or PCC); Heparin → Protamine 1 mg/100 U; LMWH → Protamine partial. Complications: hypocalcaemia, hyperkalaemia, TRALI, TACO, DIC, ARDS. PPH 4 T's: Tone (70%, atony), Trauma, Tissue (retained), Thrombin. PPH Rx: Oxytocin + Ergometrine + Carboprost + Misoprostol + TXA + balloon/B-Lynch/hysterectomy. Group O-neg until crossmatched.

26. MCQs (10)

  1. Massive transfusion definition:

A. ≥4 units RBC in 24 h

B. ≥10 units RBC in 24 h OR ≥4 units in 1 h

C. ≥20 units in 24 h

D. ≥2 units/h

  1. Damage control resuscitation ratio:

A. 1:1:2 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

B. 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

C. 2:1:1

D. 3:1:0

  1. TXA dose in major trauma:

A. 500 mg IV

B. 1 g IV within 3 h of injury, then 1 g over 8 h

C. 2 g bolus

D. 4 g infusion

  1. Fibrinogen target in major haemorrhage (non-obstetric):

A. >1.0 g/L

B. >1.5 g/L

C. >2.5 g/L

D. >3.0 g/L

  1. Calcium replacement in massive transfusion:

A. 5 mL per 4 units

B. 10 mL of 10% Ca gluconate per 4 units RBC

C. 20 mL per unit

D. Not needed

  1. Permissive hypotension target (non-TBI):

A. SBP 50-60

B. SBP 80-90 mmHg

C. SBP 100-110

D. SBP 120

  1. Warfarin reversal in major bleed:

A. Vitamin K alone

B. Vitamin K 5-10 mg IV + PCC 4-factor 25-50 U/kg

C. FFP alone

D. Protamine

  1. Dabigatran reversal:

A. Andexanet

B. PCC

C. Idarucizumab 5 g IV

D. Vitamin K

  1. PPH 4 T's include all EXCEPT:

A. Tone

B. Trauma

C. Toxins

D. Thrombin

  1. CRASH-2 trial showed TXA in trauma:

A. Increased mortality

B. Reduced all-cause mortality (when given <3 h)

C. No effect

D. Increased VTE

27. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Trauma MTP activated. First pack contents:

A. 4 RBC + 2 FFP + 1 Platelets

B. 4 RBC + 4 FFP + 1 Platelets (1:1:1)

C. 6 RBC + 2 FFP

D. 10 RBC

  1. PPH, uterine atony. First-line uterotonic:

A. Ergometrine

B. Oxytocin 5 U IV

C. Carboprost

D. Misoprostol

  1. Massive transfusion, ionised Ca drops to 0.8 mmol/L. Action:

A. Continue

B. Give 10% Ca gluconate 10 mL IV

C. Stop transfusion

D. Vitamin D

  1. ROTEM FIBTEM MCF <7 mm. Indicates need for:

A. Platelets

B. Fibrinogen (cryoprecipitate or concentrate)

C. FFP

D. RBC

  1. Trauma patient on apixaban with intracranial bleed. Best reversal:

A. Vitamin K

B. Idarucizumab

C. Andexanet alfa (or PCC 4-factor 50 U/kg)

D. Protamine

  1. Permissive hypotension contraindication:

A. Penetrating trauma

B. Traumatic brain injury (need MAP ≥80)

C. Young patient

D. Female patient

  1. PPH, oxytocin and ergometrine failed. Next uterotonic:

A. Carboprost 250 mcg IM (avoid in asthma)

B. Misoprostol

C. Surgery

D. TXA

  1. Major haemorrhage, body temperature 33°C. Action:

A. Continue

B. Active warming (Bair Hugger, fluid warmers, ambient)

C. Cool patient

D. No action needed

  1. Stored blood transfusion causing hyperkalaemia (K 6.8). Rx:

A. Calcium only

B. Insulin/dextrose + calcium + consider dialysis

C. Stop

D. Salbutamol only

  1. Fibrinogen <1.5, obstetric haemorrhage. Target:

A. >1.5

B. >2.0 g/L (higher in obstetric)

C. >2.5

D. >3.0

28. Flashcards

  • Q: Massive transfusion definition

A: ≥10 units RBC in 24 h or ≥4 units in 1 h

  • Q: DCR ratio

A: 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

  • Q: TXA dose

A: 1 g IV within 3 h, then 1 g over 8 h

  • Q: Calcium replacement

A: 10 mL 10% Ca gluconate per 4 units RBC

  • Q: Fibrinogen target

A: >1.5 g/L (obstetric >2.0)

  • Q: Permissive hypotension

A: SBP 80-90 (TBI excluded)

  • Q: Lethal triad

A: Hypothermia, Acidosis, Coagulopathy

  • Q: Warfarin reversal

A: Vitamin K + PCC

  • Q: Dabigatran reversal

A: Idarucizumab 5 g

  • Q: Apixaban reversal

A: Andexanet (or PCC)

  • Q: PPH 4 T's

A: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin

  • Q: FIBTEM MCF <7 mm

A: Fibrinogen needed

29. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — ≥10 units in 24 h or ≥4 in 1 h.

MCQ 2: B — 1:1:1 (PROPPR).

MCQ 3: B — TXA 1 g IV <3 h, then 1 g over 8 h.

MCQ 4: B — >1.5 g/L.

MCQ 5: B — 10 mL Ca gluconate per 4 units RBC.

MCQ 6: B — SBP 80-90.

MCQ 7: B — Vit K + PCC.

MCQ 8: C — Idarucizumab for dabigatran.

MCQ 9: C — "Toxins" not a T; PPH 4 T's are Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin.

MCQ 10: B — CRASH-2 reduced mortality <3 h.

SBA 1: B — 1:1:1 first pack.

SBA 2: B — Oxytocin first.

SBA 3: B — Ca gluconate.

SBA 4: B — FIBTEM MCF <7 mm = low fibrinogen.

SBA 5: C — Andexanet or PCC for apixaban.

SBA 6: B — TBI excluded.

SBA 7: A — Carboprost (avoid in asthma).

SBA 8: B — Active warming.

SBA 9: B — Insulin/dextrose + Ca + dialysis if needed.

SBA 10: B — Fibrinogen target >2.0 in obstetric.
