17. Flowchart — ICU Sedation

```mermaid

flowchart TD

A[ICU Patient] --> B[Pain Assessment BPS/NRS]

B --> C{Pain present?}

C -->|Yes| D[Analgesia: Fentanyl first-line]

C -->|No| E{Sedation needed?}

E -->|No| F[Reassess]

E -->|Yes| G[Propofol or Dexmedetomidine]

G --> H{Deep sedation needed?}

H -->|No| I[RASS 0 to -1, daily SAT/SBT]

H -->|Yes| J[RASS -3 to -4, propofol/midazolam]

J --> K{NMJ needed?}

K -->|Yes| L[Rocuronium/Cisatracurium, TOF monitor]

K -->|No| M[Continue sedation]

L --> N[Daily SAT + SBT]

M --> N

I --> O[Daily CAM-ICU]

N --> O

O --> P[Wean as soon as possible]

```

18. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Analgesia first (fentanyl); light sedation (RASS 0 to -1) for most
  • First-line sedative: propofol OR dexmedetomidine
  • Avoid benzodiazepines (deliriogenic) except: status, withdrawal, tetanus
  • Deep sedation (RASS -3 to -4) for severe ARDS, ICP
  • NMJ blockade for: severe early ARDS, ICP, tetanus, status, intubation
  • Rocuronium (sugammadex reversible); Cisatracurium in renal failure
  • TOF for NMJ monitoring (target 1-2 twitches)
  • PRIS: high-dose prolonged propofol → bradycardia, acidosis, rhabdomyolysis
  • Daily SAT + SBT + ABCDEF bundle
  • Dexmedetomidine: light, cooperative, less delirium, facilitates extubation

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • State PADIS principles
  • List first-line analgesic + sedative
  • Outline ABCDEF bundle
  • List NMJ indications
  • State PRIS risk factors and management
  • Describe TOF interpretation

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

19. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Analgesia-first principle
  • RASS target (0 to -1 most; -3 to -4 severe)
  • Fentanyl first-line
  • Propofol vs dexmedetomidine
  • Avoid benzodiazepines (except seizures, withdrawal)
  • PRIS risk factors and features
  • NMJ indications
  • TOF monitoring
  • Daily SAT + SBT

Should Know

  • ABCDEF bundle
  • Sugammadex reversal
  • Cisatracurium in renal failure
  • Suxamethonium contraindications
  • Ketamine advantages (asthma, opioid-sparing)
  • ICU-acquired weakness
  • Withdrawal syndromes
  • Midazolam uses
  • PRIS management (stop propofol, supportive)
  • Volatile anaesthetics in ICU
  • Dexmedetomidine in hyperactive delirium

Nice to Know

  • PADIS 2018 details
  • DahLBA, MIDEX, PRODEX trials
  • ROSE trial (early NMJ in ARDS)
  • AnConDa/Sedaconda
  • BPS scoring
  • ICU-acquired weakness prevention
  • ICU PTSD

20. Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

21. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • PADIS principles (analgesia first, light sedation, avoid benzos, daily SAT/SBT, monitor)
  • Pain assessment (BPS, NRS)
  • Sedation assessment (RASS, SAS, target 0 to -1 most, -3 to -4 severe)
  • Delirium screening (CAM-ICU)
  • Analgesics (fentanyl first-line)
  • Sedatives (propofol, dexmedetomidine, midazolam, ketamine)
  • NMJ blockade (indications, drugs, monitoring)
  • PRIS (risk factors, features, management)
  • Daily SAT + SBT, ABCDEF bundle
  • Withdrawal syndromes

Short Note Skeleton

  • RASS scoring
  • PRIS risk factors and management
  • NMJ indications
  • TOF interpretation
  • ABCDEF bundle
  • PRIS vs NMS

Viva One-Liners

  • "Analgesia first; RASS 0 to -1 for most patients"
  • "Fentanyl first-line analgesic"
  • "Propofol or dexmedetomidine first-line sedative"
  • "Avoid benzodiazepines unless status, withdrawal, tetanus"
  • "PRIS: high-dose propofol >48 h; bradycardia, acidosis, rhabdo"
  • "NMJ indications: ARDS, ICP, tetanus, status, intubation"
  • "Rocuronium reversed by sugammadex; cisatracurium in renal failure"
  • "TOF target 1-2 twitches"
  • "ABCDEF bundle: pain, SAT/SBT, sedation, delirium, mobility, family"
  • "Daily SAT + SBT reduce mortality"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 60-year-old ventilated for ARDS, deep sedation + cisatracurium → 24 h, prone, lung-protective vent
  • 45-year-old post-op, agitated on vent → analgesia first, then propofol or dexmedetomidine, daily SAT
  • 70-year-old delirium in ICU → dexmedetomidine, avoid benzos, ABCDEF bundle
  • Tetanus: deep sedation + NMJ blockade + magnesium + wound debridement + Ig

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • Analgesia first; RASS 0 to -1
  • Fentanyl + propofol/dex
  • Avoid benzos (except status, withdrawal, tetanus)
  • PRIS: high-dose propofol >48 h
  • NMJ: ARDS, ICP, tetanus, status
  • Rocuronium = sugammadex
  • Cisatracurium = renal failure (Hofmann)
  • TOF 1-2 twitches
  • ABCDEF bundle
  • Daily SAT + SBT

22. Summary

ICU sedation principles (PADIS 2018): Analgesia first (BPS, NRS), light sedation (RASS 0 to -1), avoid benzodiazepines, daily SAT + SBT, monitor pain + sedation + delirium, individualise. First-line analgesic: Fentanyl (haemodynamic stability, no histamine). First-line sedatives: Propofol (rapid onset, ↓ICP, watch for PRIS) or Dexmedetomidine (light, cooperative, less delirium, facilitates extubation). Midazolam reserved for status epilepticus, alcohol withdrawal, tetanus. Deep sedation (RASS -3 to -4) for severe ARDS, raised ICP. NMJ blockade indications: severe early ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <150), raised ICP, tetanus, status epilepticus, intubation, intra-op. First-line NMJ: Rocuronium (rapid, reversible with sugammadex 16 mg/kg); Cisatracurium in renal/hepatic failure (Hofmann elimination). TOF monitoring: target 1-2 twitches. PRIS: high-dose propofol (>4 mg/kg/h) for >48 h → bradycardia, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalaemia, rhabdomyolysis, Brugada-like ECG. Stop propofol, supportive (dialysis for rhabdo, pacemaker). Avoid suxamethonium in burns (>24 h), denervation, hyperkalaemia, malignant hyperthermia. ABCDEF bundle: Assess pain, Both SAT/SBT, Choice of sedation, Delirium, Early mobility, Family. Withdrawal syndromes after prolonged sedation. ICU-acquired weakness from prolonged NMJ + immobility + sepsis.

23. MCQs (10)

  1. Sugammadex reverses:

C. Cisatracurium

D. Atracurium

C. Faster onset

A. Young patient

B. Burns (>24 h), hyperkalaemia, denervation, MH

C. Diabetes

D. Old age

  1. Dexmedetomidine advantage over propofol:

A. Cheaper

B. Light sedation, less delirium, cooperative

C. Deeper sedation

D. Reversible

  1. TOF target for NMJ blockade:

A. 4 twitches

B. 1-2 twitches

C. 0 twitches

D. Pain response

  1. Daily SAT + SBT benefit:

A. Increased LOS

B. Reduced mortality and LOS

C. Increased delirium

D. No effect

24. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient on propofol 5 mg/kg/h × 3 days, bradycardic, lactate 8, K 6.5, urine myoglobin. Diagnosis:

A. Sepsis

B. PRIS

C. Malignant hyperthermia

D. NMS

A. Continue sedation

A. Patient comfortable

B. Active seizures, agitation, paralysis, ↑ICP

D. Evening

A. EEG

C. BP

D. SpO₂

25. Flashcards

26. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Fentanyl is first-line.

MCQ 2: B — RASS 0 to -1.

SBA 1: B — PRIS: high-dose prolonged propofol + bradycardia + acidosis + rhabdo.

SBA 10: C — RASS -2 = drowsy, light sedation.

topics.title: Sepsis And Septic Shock
topics.html:
Related: [[Shock - Overview]], [[Neutropenic Sepsis]], [[Toxic Shock Syndromes]]

[!important]
Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock = subset with profound circulatory and cellular/metabolic abnormalities (lactate >2 mmol/L + vasopressor requirement after adequate fluid resuscitation).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Apply the current Sepsis-3 definition
  • Recognise sepsis early using qSOFA / clinical suspicion
  • Deliver the Hour-1 Bundle
  • Understand the pathophysiology of septic shock
  • Manage fluid, vasopressors, and source control

2. Definition (Sepsis-3, 2016)

  • Sepsis: Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection (SOFA score increase ≥2)
  • Septic shock: Sepsis with persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg and lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation

3. qSOFA (quick SOFA) – Bedside Screening

≥2 of the following:

  • Respiratory rate ≥22/min
  • Altered mentation (GCS <15)
  • Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg

4. Pathophysiology

  • PAMPs + DAMPs → massive cytokine release
  • Vasodilatation + capillary leak + myocardial depression
  • Mitochondrial dysfunction + cytopathic hypoxia
  • Coagulopathy and immunosuppression (CARS)

5. Clinical Features

  • Fever or hypothermia
  • Tachycardia, tachypnoea
  • Warm then cold peripheries
  • Altered mental status
  • Oliguria, jaundice, ileus
  • Petechiae / purpura (meningococcal / DIC)

6. Hour-1 Bundle (Surviving Sepsis)

  1. Measure lactate
  2. Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics
  3. Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics
  4. Begin rapid fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotension or lactate ≥4)

7. Management

  • Fluids: 30 mL/kg crystalloid in first 3 hours (balanced solutions preferred)
  • Vasopressors: Noradrenaline first-line (target MAP ≥65 mmHg)
  • Source control: Within 6–12 hours (surgical drainage, removal of infected devices)
  • Antibiotics: Within 1 hour — broad spectrum, de-escalate later
  • Steroids: Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day if vasopressor-dependent
  • Blood products: Hb target 70 g/L (unless active bleeding or ischaemia)

8. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  • Refractory hypotension
  • DIC, ARDS, AKI
  • Meningococcal sepsis (rapid purpura fulminans)

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Sepsis can present with normal temperature (especially elderly/immunocompromised)
  • Lactate clearance is a key resuscitation target
  • Early source control is as important as antibiotics
  • Do not delay antibiotics for imaging or cultures if patient is unstable

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define sepsis and septic shock (Sepsis-3).
  2. What is the Hour-1 Bundle?
  3. Which vasopressor is first-line in septic shock?
  4. When would you give steroids in sepsis?
  5. How do you approach source control?

11. One-Page Revision Summary

Sepsis = infection + organ dysfunction

  • Screen with qSOFA
  • Hour-1 Bundle: lactate + cultures + antibiotics + 30 mL/kg fluid
  • Noradrenaline for refractory hypotension
  • Source control within hours

12. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Sepsis-3 definitions (infection + SOFA ≥2; septic shock = vasopressor + lactate >2 after fluids)
  • qSOFA ≥2 (RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100) for screening
  • Hour-1 Bundle: lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors
  • Noradrenaline first-line vasopressor (MAP ≥65)
  • Source control within 6–12h (drain abscess, remove line, surgery)

Should Know

  • Lactate clearance ≥10–20% per 2h as resuscitation target
  • Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day if vasopressor-dependent
  • Hb target 70 g/L (restrictive transfusion)
  • Sepsis can present with normothermia/hypothermia (esp. elderly)

Nice to Know

  • Procalcitonin for antibiotic stewardship
  • Dynamic fluid responsiveness (PLR, SVV)
  • ECMO in refractory septic shock
  • Long-term outcomes: PICS, cognitive/physical impairment

13. MCQs (10)

  1. Sepsis-3 definition of sepsis:

A. Infection + SIRS ≥2

B. Infection + SOFA score increase ≥2

C. Infection + qSOFA ≥2

D. Bacteremia + organ dysfunction

  1. Sepsis-3 septic shock definition:

A. SBP <90 + lactate >2

B. Vasopressor for MAP ≥65 + lactate >2 after 30 mL/kg fluid

C. SBP <90 + lactate >4

D. Vasopressor + lactate >4

  1. qSOFA criteria (score 1 each):

A. RR≥20, GCS<14, SBP<110

B. RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100

C. RR≥24, GCS<13, SBP<90

D. RR≥30, GCS<12, SBP<80

  1. Hour-1 Bundle — which is NOT included?

A. Measure lactate

B. Blood cultures before antibiotics

C. Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h

D. Central line insertion

  1. First-line vasopressor in septic shock:

A. Adrenaline

B. Vasopressin

C. Noradrenaline

D. Dopamine

  1. Target MAP in septic shock (Surviving Sepsis):

A. ≥60 mmHg

B. ≥65 mmHg

C. ≥70 mmHg

D. ≥75 mmHg

  1. Fluid bolus in sepsis for hypotension/lactate ≥4:

A. 10 mL/kg

B. 20 mL/kg

C. 30 mL/kg crystalloid

D. 50 mL/kg

  1. When to add vasopressin to noradrenaline:

A. Immediately

B. When noradrenaline ~0.25–0.5 µg/kg/min (catecholamine-sparing)

C. Only when noradrenaline >2 µg/kg/min

D. Never

  1. Hydrocortisone indication in septic shock:

A. All septic shock

B. Vasopressor-dependent shock

C. Lactate >4 only

D. First 6 hours only

  1. Lactate clearance target:

A. ≥5% per hour

B. ≥10–20% per 2 hours

C. Normalise in 1 hour

D. <1 mmol/L in 6 hours

14. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 65-year-old man with pneumonia, BP 75/40 after 2L fluid, lactate 5.2. On noradrenaline 0.3 µg/kg/min. Next step:

A. Give another 2L fluid

B. Add vasopressin 0.03 U/min

C. Switch to adrenaline

D. Stop noradrenaline

  1. A 70-year-old woman with septic shock, noradrenaline 0.8 µg/kg/min + vasopressin 0.04 U/min, MAP 60, lactate 6 (was 5 at 2h). Echo shows LVEF 25%, dilated LV. Best addition:

A. More fluid

B. Dobutamine (for myocardial depression)

C. Adrenaline

D. Hydrocortisone

  1. Assessing fluid responsiveness in ventilated septic patient. Best method:

A. CVP >12

B. SVV >13% (if sinus rhythm, TV ≥8 mL/kg)

C. Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr

D. MAP response to 100 mL fluid

  1. A patient with septic shock from cholangitis. Source control timeline:

A. ERCP within 24 hours

B. ERCP within 6 hours

C. Antibiotics only

D. Surgery immediately

  1. Hydrocortisone dose in septic shock:

A. 50 mg/day

B. 100 mg/day

C. 200 mg/day

D. 400 mg/day

  1. A septic patient with "cold shock" (cool peripheries, low CO). Most appropriate:

A. Noradrenaline only

B. Noradrenaline + dobutamine

C. Vasopressin only

D. Adrenaline only

  1. Lactate clearance target:

A. ≥5% per hour

B. ≥10–20% per 2 hours

C. Normalise in 6 hours

D. <2 mmol/L in 1 hour

  1. In septic shock, blood transfusion threshold (no active bleed/ischaemia):

A. Hb <70 g/L

B. Hb <80 g/L

C. Hb <90 g/L

D. Hb <100 g/L

  1. Angiotensin II is used for:

A. First-line septic shock

B. Refractory shock despite catecholamines + vasopressin

C. Cardiogenic shock

D. Anaphylaxis

  1. Best initial fluid in septic shock (SSC):

A. 0.9% saline

B. Balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's/Plasma-Lyte)

C. Albumin 5%

D. Gelatin

15. Flashcards

  • Q: Sepsis-3 sepsis definition

A: Infection + SOFA increase ≥2

  • Q: Sepsis-3 septic shock

A: Vasopressor for MAP ≥65 + lactate >2 AFTER 30 mL/kg crystalloid

  • Q: Hour-1 Bundle

A: Lactate, Cultures, Antibiotics, 30 mL/kg Fluid, Vasopressors

  • Q: qSOFA

A: RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100 (≥2 = positive)

  • Q: First-line vasopressor

A: Noradrenaline (start peripherally early)

  • Q: Vasopressin dose

A: 0.01–0.04 U/min (add-on at NA ~0.25–0.5)

  • Q: Fluid responsiveness test

A: PLR + CO monitoring (gold); SVV/PPV if ventilated

  • Q: Hydrocortisone indication

A: Vasopressor-dependent septic shock — 200 mg/day

  • Q: Source control

A: Drainable foci ≤6h; necrotising fasciitis = immediate surgery

  • Q: Refractory septic shock

A: NA >0.5 + vasopressin + still hypotensive → adrenaline, hydrocortisone, consider ECMO

16. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Exact Sepsis-3 definition.

MCQ 2: B — Exact Sepsis-3 septic shock definition.

MCQ 3: B — qSOFA: RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100.

MCQ 4: D — Central line not in Hour-1 Bundle.

MCQ 5: C — Noradrenaline first-line.

MCQ 6: B — MAP ≥65 mmHg.

MCQ 7: C — 30 mL/kg crystalloid.

MCQ 8: B — Add at NA ~0.25–0.5 as catecholamine-sparing.

MCQ 9: B — Hydrocortisone for vasopressor-dependent shock.

MCQ 10: B — ≥10–20% per 2 hours.

SBA 1: B — On NA 0.3, add vasopressin (standard ladder).

SBA 2: B — Hypodynamic phenotype (low EF) → dobutamine for inotropy.

SBA 3: B — SVV >13% dynamic index (requires conditions).

SBA 4: A — Cholangitis: ERCP ≤24h (unless unstable).

SBA 5: C — 200 mg/day IV.

SBA 6: B — Cold shock = hypodynamic → NA + dobutamine.

SBA 7: B — ≥10–20% per 2 hours.

SBA 8: A — Restrictive Hb <70.

SBA 9: B — Refractory shock salvage.

SBA 10: B — Balanced crystalloids per SSC.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Septic Shock
topics.html:
Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Distributive Shock]], [[Inotropes and Vasopressors]], [[Fluid Resuscitation Strategies]], [[Critical Care Monitoring]]

[!important]
Septic shock = subset of sepsis with profound circulatory and cellular/metabolic abnormalities carrying >40% mortality. Defined by: vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Apply Sepsis-3 septic shock definition
  • Execute the Hour-1 Bundle and 3-hour/6-hour bundles
  • Titrate vasopressors and inotropes to haemodynamic targets
  • Apply fluid responsiveness concepts to avoid overload
  • Recognise and manage refractory septic shock

2. Definition (Sepsis-3, 2016)

Septic shock = Sepsis with:

  1. Persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg
  2. Serum lactate >2 mmol/L (18 mg/dL)

Despite adequate volume resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid)

Mortality: Hospital mortality ~40–50%

3. Haemodynamic Phenotypes

PhenotypeCharacteristicsPrevalence
Hyperdynamic (classic)High CO, low SVR, warm peripheries~70%
HypodynamicLow CO, low SVR (myocardial depression)~30%
VasoplegicRefractory hypotension, very low SVRSubset

Bedside echo is essential to differentiate — guides inotrope vs vasopressor choice.

4. Hour-1 Bundle (Surviving Sepsis 2021)

  1. Measure lactate (repeat if >2 mmol/L)
  2. Blood cultures before antibiotics (2 sets + line cultures if present)
  3. Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour
  4. 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  5. Vasopressors if hypotensive during/after fluid resuscitation (target MAP ≥65)

5. Vasopressor Strategy

AgentDoseRoleKey Points
Noradrenaline0.01–2 µg/kg/minFirst-lineα₁ > β₁; increases MAP, minimal CO effect
Vasopressin0.01–0.04 U/minAdd-on (2nd line)Non-catecholamine; spares noradrenaline; may improve renal perfusion
Adrenaline0.01–1 µg/kg/min2nd/3rd lineα + β₁ + β₂; increases CO but ↑ lactate, arrhythmias
Angiotensin II2.5–80 ng/kg/minRefractoryAT1 receptor; trials show MAP improvement

Titration: Start noradrenaline early (even peripherally for short-term) — do not wait for central line.

6. Fluid Responsiveness in Septic Shock

  • Only ~50% are fluid responders
  • Dynamic measures > static:
  • Passive Leg Raise (PLR) + CO monitoring (gold standard)
  • SVV / PPV (requires: sedated, ventilated, sinus rhythm, TV ≥8 mL/kg)
  • Mini-fluid challenge (100 mL over 1 min)
  • Stop fluids if no response → escalate vasopressors

7. Inotrope Use (if myocardial depression)

  • Dobutamine 2.5–20 µg/kg/min (β₁ > β₂) — increases CO, may ↓ MAP (add noradrenaline)
  • Levosimendan (Ca²⁺ sensitiser + PDE inhibitor) — alternative, longer half-life
  • Echo-guided: LVEF <30% or new wall motion abnormality + low CO

8. Adjunctive Therapies

TherapyIndicationEvidence
Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day IVVasopressor-dependent septic shock↓ Vasopressor duration, ?mortality
IVIGConsider in streptococcal/staphylococcal TSSLow certainty
Blood productsHb <70 (or <80 if ischaemia)Restrictive strategy
Renal replacementStandard AKI indicationsNo early benefit

9. Source Control — Critical Timeline

SourceInterventionTarget Time
Infected lineRemoveImmediately
AbscessDrain (percut/surgical)≤6 hours
CholangitisERCP≤24 hours
Necrotising fasciitisDebridementImmediate
Perforated viscusSurgeryImmediate

10. Refractory Septic Shock Definition

  • MAP <65 mmHg despite noradrenaline ≥0.5 µg/kg/min (+ vasopressin)
  • Or lactate >4 mmol/L not clearing
  • Or new organ failure despite support

Escalation:

  1. Add vasopressin 0.03 U/min
  2. Consider adrenaline (if low CO)
  3. Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day
  4. Consider angiotensin II (if available)
  5. ECMO (selected centres)

11. Red Flags

  • Lactate not clearing (>10–20% per 2h)
  • Rising noradrenaline dose >1 µg/kg/min
  • New oliguria / anuria
  • Ischaemic limbs / gut (vasopressor toxicity)

12. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Septic shock definition: vasopressor + lactate >2 AFTER 30 mL/kg fluid
  • Noradrenaline first-line — start early, peripherally if needed
  • Vasopressin add-on at noradrenaline ~0.25–0.5 µg/kg/min (spares catecholamines)
  • Fluid responsiveness must be tested — avoid fluid overload
  • Source control within 6h for drainable foci
  • Hydrocortisone if vasopressor-dependent
  • Echo to differentiate hyperdynamic vs hypodynamic

13. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define septic shock (Sepsis-3).
  2. What is the Hour-1 Bundle?
  3. When do you add vasopressin to noradrenaline?
  4. How do you assess fluid responsiveness?
  5. What is the role of hydrocortisone?
  6. When do you use dobutamine in septic shock?

14. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Giving 30 mL/kg fluid to all septic patients (some need less, some more — assess responsiveness)
  • Delaying vasopressors waiting for central line
  • Assuming "high CO" = no need for inotropes (myocardial depression still possible)
  • Confusing sepsis (infection + organ dysfunction) with septic shock (subset with circulatory failure)

15. Mnemonics

  • SEPSIS-3: SOFA ≥2 + infection
  • SEPTIC SHOCK: Vasopressor + Lactate >2 + Fluids
  • Hour-1: Lactate + Cultures + Antibiotics + Fluids + Vasopressors
  • Vasopressor ladder: Noradrenaline → +Vasopressin → +Adrenaline → Hydrocortisone → Angiotensin II/ECMO

16. One-Page Revision Summary

Septic Shock = Vasopressor + Lactate >2 after 30 mL/kg

  • Hour-1: Lactate, Cultures, Abx, Fluids, Vasopressors
  • Noradrenaline first (start peripherally early)
  • Test fluid responsiveness (PLR, SVV/PPV)
  • Vasopressin add-on at ~0.25–0.5 µg/kg/min NA
  • Echo: hyperdynamic vs hypodynamic
  • Source control ≤6h
  • Hydrocortisone if vasopressor-dependent

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Sepsis-3 septic shock definition
  • Hour-1 Bundle
  • Noradrenaline first-line
  • Fluid responsiveness testing
  • Vasopressin as add-on
  • Source control timeline

Should Know

  • Hypodynamic vs hyperdynamic phenotypes
  • Dobutamine for myocardial depression
  • Hydrocortisone indication
  • Refractory shock escalation

Nice to Know

  • Angiotensin II, IVIG, levosimendan
  • ECMO criteria
  • Biomarkers (procalcitonin, presepsin)

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Sepsis-3 septic shock definition requires:

A. SBP <90 + lactate >2

B. Vasopressor for MAP ≥65 + lactate >2 after 30 mL/kg fluid

C. SBP <90 + lactate >4

D. Vasopressor + lactate >4

  1. First-line vasopressor in septic shock:

A. Adrenaline

B. Vasopressin

C. Noradrenaline

D. Dopamine

  1. Target MAP in septic shock (SSC):

A. ≥60 mmHg

B. ≥65 mmHg

C. ≥70 mmHg

D. ≥75 mmHg

  1. Vasopressin dose in septic shock:

A. 0.01–0.04 U/min

B. 0.1–0.4 U/min

C. 1–4 U/min

D. 10–40 U/min

  1. Best dynamic measure of fluid responsiveness:

A. CVP

B. Passive leg raise with CO monitoring

C. Urine output

D. Blood pressure

  1. Hydrocortisone indication in septic shock:

A. All septic shock

B. Vasopressor-dependent shock

C. Lactate >4 only

D. First 6 hours only

  1. When to start antibiotics in septic shock:

A. After CT scan

B. After blood cultures only

C. Within 1 hour (Hour-1 Bundle)

D. After source identification

  1. Fluid bolus in SSC guidelines for hypotension/lactate ≥4:

A. 10 mL/kg

B. 20 mL/kg

C. 30 mL/kg crystalloid

D. 50 mL/kg

  1. Noradrenaline can be started:

A. Only via central line

B. Peripherally (short-term) while central access obtained

C. Only after 3L fluid

D. Only in ICU

  1. Refractory septic shock = noradrenaline >0.5 µg/kg/min + vasopressin +:

A. MAP <65

B. Lactate not clearing

C. New organ failure

D. All of the above

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 65-year-old man with pneumonia, BP 75/40 after 2L fluid, lactate 5.2. On noradrenaline 0.3 µg/kg/min. Next step:

A. Give another 2L fluid

B. Add vasopressin 0.03 U/min

C. Switch to adrenaline

D. Stop noradrenaline

  1. A 70-year-old woman with septic shock, noradrenaline 0.8 µg/kg/min + vasopressin 0.04 U/min, MAP 60, lactate 6 (was 5 at 2h). Echo shows LVEF 25%, dilated LV. Best addition:

A. More fluid

B. Dobutamine

C. Adrenaline

D. Hydrocortisone

  1. Assessing fluid responsiveness in ventilated septic patient. Best method:

A. CVP >12

B. SVV >13% (if sinus rhythm, TV ≥8 mL/kg)

C. Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr

D. MAP response to 100 mL fluid

  1. A patient with septic shock from cholangitis. Source control timeline:

A. ERCP within 24 hours

B. ERCP within 6 hours

C. Antibiotics only

D. Surgery immediately

  1. Hydrocortisone dose in septic shock:

A. 50 mg/day

B. 100 mg/day

C. 200 mg/day

D. 400 mg/day

  1. A septic patient with "cold shock" (cool peripheries, low CO). Most appropriate:

A. Noradrenaline only

B. Noradrenaline + dobutamine

C. Vasopressin only

D. Adrenaline only

  1. Lactate clearance target:

A. ≥5% per hour

B. ≥10–20% per 2 hours

C. Normalise in 6 hours

D. <2 mmol/L in 1 hour

  1. In septic shock, blood transfusion threshold (no active bleed/ischaemia):

A. Hb <70 g/L

B. Hb <80 g/L

C. Hb <90 g/L

D. Hb <100 g/L

  1. Angiotensin II is used for:

A. First-line septic shock

B. Refractory shock despite catecholamines + vasopressin

C. Cardiogenic shock

D. Anaphylaxis

  1. Best initial fluid in septic shock (SSC):

A. 0.9% saline

B. Balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's/Plasma-Lyte)

C. Albumin 5%

D. Gelatin

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Septic shock definition (Sepsis-3)

A: Vasopressor for MAP ≥65 + lactate >2 AFTER 30 mL/kg crystalloid

  • Q: Hour-1 Bundle

A: Lactate, Cultures, Antibiotics, 30 mL/kg Fluid, Vasopressors

  • Q: First-line vasopressor

A: Noradrenaline (start peripherally early)

  • Q: Vasopressin dose

A: 0.01–0.04 U/min (add-on at NA ~0.25–0.5)

  • Q: Fluid responsiveness test

A: PLR + CO monitoring (gold); SVV/PPV if ventilated

  • Q: Hydrocortisone indication

A: Vasopressor-dependent septic shock — 200 mg/day

  • Q: Dobutamine in septic shock

A: If myocardial depression (low EF, low CO) — may need NA combo

  • Q: Source control

A: Drainable foci ≤6h; necrotising fasciitis = immediate surgery

  • Q: Refractory septic shock

A: NA >0.5 + vasopressin + still hypotensive → adrenaline, hydrocortisone, consider ECMO

  • Q: Balanced crystalloids vs saline

A: Balanced preferred (less hyperchloraemic acidosis, less AKI)

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Exact Sepsis-3 definition.

MCQ 2: C — Noradrenaline first-line.

MCQ 3: B — MAP ≥65 mmHg.

MCQ 4: A — 0.01–0.04 U/min.

MCQ 5: B — PLR + CO is gold standard dynamic test.

MCQ 6: B — Hydrocortisone for vasopressor-dependent shock.

MCQ 7: C — Within 1 hour.

MCQ 8: C — 30 mL/kg crystalloid.

MCQ 9: B — Peripheral start acceptable short-term.

MCQ 10: D — All define refractory shock.

SBA 1: B — On NA 0.3, add vasopressin (standard ladder).

SBA 2: B — Hypodynamic phenotype (low EF) → dobutamine for inotropy.

SBA 3: B — SVV >13% dynamic index (requires conditions).

SBA 4: A — Cholangitis: ERCP ≤24h (unless unstable).

SBA 5: C — 200 mg/day IV.

SBA 6: B — Cold shock = hypodynamic → NA + dobutamine.

SBA 7: B — ≥10–20% per 2 hours.

SBA 8: A — Restrictive Hb <70.

SBA 9: B — Refractory shock salvage.

SBA 10: B — Balanced crystalloids per SSC.

topics.title: Shock Overview
topics.html:
Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Hypovolaemic Shock]], [[Cardiogenic Shock]], [[Obstructive Shock]], [[Distributive Shock]]

[!important]
Shock = inadequate tissue perfusion and oxygenation despite adequate circulating volume or cardiac output. It is a time-critical emergency — early recognition and resuscitation save lives.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define shock and classify its types
  • Understand the common final pathway of cellular hypoxia
  • Apply a rapid clinical approach to any shocked patient
  • Recognise compensated vs decompensated shock
  • Initiate immediate resuscitation while identifying the underlying cause

2. Definition

Shock is a state of acute circulatory failure leading to inadequate tissue perfusion and cellular hypoxia, resulting in cellular dysfunction and, if untreated, organ failure and death.

Key concept: Blood pressure may be normal in early/compensated shock.

3. Core Physiology

  • Oxygen delivery (DO₂) = Cardiac Output (CO) × Arterial Oxygen Content (CaO₂)
  • CO = Heart Rate (HR) × Stroke Volume (SV)
  • SV depends on preload, afterload, and contractility
  • Shock occurs when DO₂ is insufficient to meet tissue oxygen demand

4. Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

  • Lactate: >2 mmol/L → tissue hypoperfusion (≥4 mmol/L = severe)
  • MAP target in septic shock: ≥65 mmHg
  • ScvO₂ target: ≥70%
  • Urine output: ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr
  • Base deficit: >6 → significant hypoperfusion

5. Classification

TypeMechanismExamplesSkinCVPCO
HypovolaemicReduced preloadHaemorrhage, dehydrationCool, clammyLowLow
CardiogenicPump failureMI, cardiomyopathy, arrhythmiaCool, clammyHighLow
ObstructiveMechanical obstructionPE, tamponade, tension PTXCool, clammyHighLow
DistributiveVasodilatation + capillary leakSepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenicWarmLowHigh
SepticDistributive + myocardial depressionGram-negative / positive sepsisWarm → coldLowHigh → Low

6. Etiology / Causes

See individual shock type notes.

7. Pathophysiology (Common Final Pathway)

  1. Inadequate perfusion → cellular hypoxia
  2. Anaerobic metabolism → lactate production
  3. Cellular swelling, mitochondrial dysfunction
  4. Inflammatory cascade activation
  5. Endothelial damage, capillary leak
  6. Worsening hypovolaemia and myocardial depression (vicious cycle)
  7. Irreversible cell death and multi-organ failure

8. Clinical Features

Compensated shock (early)

  • Tachycardia, tachypnoea
  • Narrow pulse pressure
  • Cool peripheries, delayed capillary refill
  • Anxiety, mild confusion
  • Normal or slightly low BP

Decompensated shock (late)

  • Hypotension
  • Altered mental status
  • Oliguria / anuria
  • Cold, mottled skin
  • Metabolic acidosis, high lactate

9. Approach / Algorithm (ABCDE + Source Control)

  1. Airway — protect if GCS low
  2. Breathing — high-flow O₂, consider intubation
  3. Circulation — 2 large-bore IV cannulae, bloods, lactate, blood gas
  4. Disability — AVPU/GCS, blood glucose
  5. Exposure — full exposure, look for source (rash, wound, abdomen, legs)

Simultaneously:

  • Fluid challenge (250–500 mL crystalloid)
  • Identify type of shock clinically + bedside echo/US
  • Early source control (antibiotics, drainage, surgery)

10. Investigations

  • Immediate: VBG/ABG + lactate, FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation, glucose, troponin, ECG, CXR
  • Targeted: Echo (bedside), CT (if stable), cultures, procalcitonin, etc.

11. Interpretation Frameworks

  • Lactate clearance: aim for ≥10–20% reduction every 2 hours
  • ScvO₂ or SvO₂: low in most shocks except distributive (may be high)

12. Management (General Principles)

  • Oxygen — target SpO₂ 94–98% (or 88–92% in COPD)
  • Fluids — crystalloids first (balanced solutions preferred)
  • Vasopressors — start early if fluid-refractory (noradrenaline first-line in most cases)
  • Inotropes — dobutamine if low cardiac output
  • Source control — within 1–3 hours for sepsis
  • Monitoring — arterial line, central line, urine output, lactate

13. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  • Hypotension not responding to 1–2 L fluid
  • Altered consciousness
  • Signs of tamponade, massive PE, or tension pneumothorax

14. Prognosis

  • Mortality rises sharply with duration of shock and number of organs failing
  • Early goal-directed therapy + source control significantly improves survival

15. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Shock can exist with normal blood pressure (especially in young patients)
  • Warm peripheries + high CO classically point to distributive shock
  • Always measure lactate — it is the single best bedside marker of tissue hypoperfusion
  • In any shocked patient, think "Is this hypovolaemic, cardiogenic, obstructive, or distributive?"

16. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define shock. How do you classify it?
  2. What is the difference between compensated and decompensated shock?
  3. Why can BP be normal in early shock?
  4. What is the role of lactate in shock management?
  5. Outline your immediate management of a shocked patient.

17. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Assuming hypotension is required for diagnosis of shock
  • Treating all shock with large-volume fluids (dangerous in cardiogenic/obstructive)
  • Delaying antibiotics/source control while chasing "more fluids"

18. One-Page Revision Summary

Shock = inadequate tissue perfusion

  • Classify by mechanism (4 main types)
  • Use ABCDE + lactate + bedside echo
  • Resuscitate while identifying cause
  • Source control is as important as haemodynamics

19. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Definition and 4-type classification
  • Clinical differentiation of shock types
  • Lactate as key marker
  • ABCDE resuscitation sequence

Should Know

  • Pathophysiology of cellular hypoxia
  • Vasopressor choice by shock type
  • Lactate clearance targets

Nice to Know

  • Detailed haemodynamic profiles (CO, SVR, PCWP)
  • Advanced monitoring (PiCCO, PA catheter)

20. MCQs (10)

  1. Which shock type typically presents with warm peripheries and bounding pulses?

A. Hypovolaemic

B. Cardiogenic

C. Distributive

D. Obstructive

  1. Most sensitive bedside marker of tissue hypoperfusion:

A. Blood pressure

B. Heart rate

C. Serum lactate

D. Urine output

  1. Shock can exist with:

A. Always with hypotension

B. Always with tachycardia

C. Normal blood pressure (compensated)

D. Always with bradycardia

  1. MAP target in septic shock resuscitation:

A. ≥55 mmHg

B. ≥65 mmHg

C. ≥75 mmHg

D. ≥85 mmHg

  1. Base deficit >6 indicates:

A. Normal

B. Mild hypoperfusion

C. Significant hypoperfusion / occult shock

D. Respiratory alkalosis

  1. Cool, clammy skin + flat neck veins + tachycardia suggests:

A. Distributive shock

B. Hypovolaemic shock

C. Cardiogenic shock with pulmonary oedema

D. Obstructive shock

  1. First fluid of choice in hypovolaemic shock (non-haemorrhagic):

A. 5% albumin

B. Gelatin

C. Balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's/Plasma-Lyte)

D. 0.9% saline only

  1. Lactate clearance target in shock:

A. 5% per hour

B. ≥10–20% per 2 hours

C. Normal in 30 min

D. <1 mmol/L in 6 hours

  1. ScvO₂ target in early goal-directed therapy:

A. ≥50%

B. ≥60%

C. ≥70%

D. ≥80%

  1. Urine output target in shock resuscitation:

A. >10 mL/hr

B. >0.5 mL/kg/hr

C. >2 mL/kg/hr

D. >5 mL/kg/hr

21. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 68-year-old man with pneumonia becomes hypotensive (BP 85/50), tachycardic (HR 130), and has warm peripheries with a lactate of 5.2. Which is the most likely shock type?

A. Hypovolaemic

B. Cardiogenic

C. Septic (distributive)

D. Obstructive

  1. A 30-year-old trauma patient with HR 140, BP 80/50, cool peripheries, flat neck veins, lactate 8. Best initial fluid:

A. 0.9% saline 5 L

B. 5% albumin

C. Balanced crystalloid bolus, then blood products (1:1:1) early

D. Noradrenaline

  1. A 55-year-old with anterior STEMI, BP 75/50, HR 120, cold clammy, JVP elevated, basal crackles. Diagnosis:

A. Hypovolaemic shock

B. Cardiogenic shock

C. Septic shock

D. Distributive shock

  1. A 70-year-old post-op, sudden hypotension 70/40, tachycardia 130, raised JVP, muffled heart sounds. Diagnosis:

A. Massive PE

B. Cardiac tamponade

C. Pneumothorax

D. Hypovolaemia

  1. A young trauma patient, hypotension, trachea deviated to right, absent breath sounds left. Immediate management:

A. Chest drain

B. Needle decompression (2nd ICS midclavicular) then chest drain

C. CT chest

D. Fluid bolus

  1. Best bedside test to differentiate shock types in ED:

A. CT chest

B. Bedside echocardiography

C. MRI

D. Lactate alone

  1. A septic shock patient on noradrenaline 0.5 mcg/kg/min still MAP 60. Next step:

A. Stop vasopressors

B. Add vasopressin or increase noradrenaline; target MAP ≥65

C. Hydroxocobalamin

D. Adrenaline infusion

  1. Source control in septic shock from abdominal abscess — timing:

A. Within 24 h

B. Within 1–3 hours (or as soon as feasible)

C. After antibiotics only

D. Never

  1. A patient with anaphylaxis, BP 70/40, wheeze. First-line treatment:

A. Adrenaline 0.5 mg IV

B. Adrenaline 0.5 mg IM (anterolateral thigh)

C. Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV

D. Saline 5 L

  1. Persistent shock despite 2 L crystalloid in 30 min. Next step:

A. More fluid only

B. Start vasopressors early + identify type of shock

C. Stop fluids

D. CT scan

22. Flashcards

  • Q: Define shock

A: Acute circulatory failure causing inadequate tissue perfusion and cellular hypoxia

  • Q: Most sensitive bedside marker of tissue hypoperfusion

A: Serum lactate

  • Q: 4 main shock types

A: Hypovolaemic, Cardiogenic, Obstructive, Distributive

  • Q: MAP target in septic shock

A: ≥65 mmHg

  • Q: ScvO₂ target

A: ≥70%

  • Q: Urine output target

A: ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr

  • Q: Lactate clearance target

A: ≥10–20% per 2 hours

  • Q: Warm peripheries + high CO = ?

A: Distributive (septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic)

  • Q: Cool peripheries + low JVP = ?

A: Hypovolaemic shock

  • Q: Cool peripheries + high JVP = ?

A: Cardiogenic OR obstructive

  • Q: Base deficit >6 means

A: Significant hypoperfusion / occult shock

  • Q: First fluid in hypovolaemic shock (non-haemorrhagic)

A: Balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's / Plasma-Lyte)

23. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: C — Distributive shock causes vasodilatation → warm peripheries.

MCQ 2: C — Lactate is the best single marker of tissue hypoperfusion.

MCQ 3: C — BP can be normal in early/compensated shock.

MCQ 4: B — MAP target ≥65 mmHg in septic shock (Surviving Sepsis).

MCQ 5: C — Base deficit >6 = significant hypoperfusion.

MCQ 6: B — Cool + flat neck veins = hypovolaemic.

MCQ 7: C — Balanced crystalloid preferred (Plasma-Lyte/Hartmann's).

MCQ 8: B — Lactate clearance ≥10–20% per 2 hours.

MCQ 9: C — ScvO₂ ≥70% (EGDT target).

MCQ 10: B — UO target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr.

SBA 1: C — Pneumonia + warm peripheries + high lactate = septic shock.

SBA 2: C — Trauma + shock → balanced crystalloid bolus + early blood products.

SBA 3: B — STEMI + hypotension + raised JVP + crackles = cardiogenic shock.

SBA 4: B — Beck's triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, raised JVP) = tamponade.

SBA 5: B — Tension pneumothorax → immediate needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL.

SBA 6: B — Bedside echo rapidly differentiates shock types.

SBA 7: B — Septic shock on noradrenaline with low MAP → escalate/add vasopressin.

SBA 8: B — Source control within 1–3 hours.

SBA 9: B — Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (anterolateral thigh).

SBA 10: B — Persistent shock → start vasopressors + identify type.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-05

topics.title: Status Epilepticus
topics.html:
Related: [[Coma and Altered Consciousness]], [[ABCDE Approach and Initial Assessment]]

[!important]
Status epilepticus is a neurological emergency. Treatment must start within 5 minutes. Mortality rises sharply after 30 minutes.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define status epilepticus
  • Apply time-based treatment protocol
  • Know first-, second-, and third-line drugs
  • Manage complications (airway, rhabdomyolysis, acidosis)

2. Definition

Continuous seizure activity >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without recovery of consciousness.

3. Time-Based Management (UK Protocol)

0–5 min: ABCDE + glucose + benzodiazepine (lorazepam 4 mg IV or midazolam 10 mg buccal)

5–20 min: Repeat benzodiazepine once

20–40 min: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV or levetiracetam 60 mg/kg

40–60 min: Phenobarbital or general anaesthesia (propofol/thiopental) + ICU

4. Key Investigations

  • Glucose, electrolytes, ABG, lactate, CK
  • CT/MRI brain (after stabilisation)
  • EEG (if non-convulsive status suspected)

5. Red Flags / Complications

  • Airway compromise
  • Rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalaemia, acidosis
  • Cerebral oedema, permanent neuronal damage

6. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Lorazepam is preferred IV benzodiazepine
  • Treat aggressively — neuronal damage begins after 20–30 minutes
  • Always check glucose first

7. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define status epilepticus and outline time-based management.
  2. What is first-line treatment?
  3. When would you intubate?

8. One-Page Revision Summary

0–5 min: Benzodiazepine

5–20 min: Repeat benzo

20–40 min: Phenytoin / Levetiracetam

>40 min: Anaesthesia + ICU

9. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Definition: continuous seizure >5 min OR recurrent without recovery
  • Time-based protocol: 0–5 (benzo), 5–20 (repeat benzo), 20–40 (phenytoin/levetiracetam), >40 (anaesthesia+ICU)
  • First-line: lorazepam 4mg IV (or midazolam 10mg buccal/IM)
  • Always check glucose FIRST
  • Intubate if GCS ≤8 or airway compromised

Should Know

  • Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV (max 2g) OR levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (preferred — fewer side effects)
  • Third-line: phenobarbital or anaesthetic agents (propofol/thiopental/midazolam infusion)
  • Complications: rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalaemia, acidosis, aspiration pneumonia
  • Non-convulsive status: EEG diagnosis (subtle: eye deviation, automatisms)

Nice to Know

  • Refractory status: continuous EEG monitoring, ketamine, lacosamide, magnesium
  • Febrile status epilepticus in children
  • Newer agents: brivaracetam, perampanel
  • Long-term outcomes: cognitive, epilepsy risk

10. MCQs (10)

  1. Status epilepticus definition (current):

A. Seizure >10 min

B. Seizure >5 min OR recurrent without recovery

C. Seizure >30 min

D. >3 seizures in 1 hour

  1. First-line treatment (0–5 min):

A. Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV

B. Lorazepam 4 mg IV (or midazolam 10 mg buccal)

C. Phenobarbital

D. Propofol infusion

  1. Second-line (5–20 min) if seizure continues:

A. Levetiracetam

B. Repeat benzodiazepine ONCE

C. Phenytoin

D. Intubate

  1. Third-line (20–40 min):

A. Repeat benzo again

B. Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV OR levetiracetam 60 mg/kg

C. Propofol infusion

D. Magnesium

  1. Preferred IV benzodiazepine:

A. Diazepam

B. Midazolam

C. Lorazepam (longer duration, less respiratory depression)

D. Clonazepam

  1. Levetiracetam dose in status epilepticus:

A. 20 mg/kg

B. 40 mg/kg

C. 60 mg/kg (max 4.5g)

D. 100 mg/kg

  1. Non-convulsive status epilepticus diagnosed by:

A. Clinical observation

B. CT head

C. EEG

D. MRI brain

  1. Complications of prolonged status include ALL EXCEPT:

A. Rhabdomyolysis

B. Hyperkalaemia

C. Hypertension (usually hypotension)

D. Metabolic acidosis

  1. Refractory status epilepticus — anaesthetic agent:

A. Lorazepam infusion

B. Propofol / thiopental / midazolam infusion + ICU

C. Phenytoin infusion

D. Levetiracetam infusion

  1. Phenytoin IV — key precautions:

A. Give rapidly

B. Cardiac monitoring, avoid extravasation (purple glove syndrome), max 50 mg/min

C. No monitoring needed

D. Give IM

  1. Treat aggressively because neuronal damage begins after:

A. 10 minutes

B. 20–30 minutes

C. 1 hour

D. 2 hours

11. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old known epileptic, seizure for 10 minutes, IV access available. Next:

A. Wait for spontaneous stop

B. Lorazepam 4 mg IV (first-line)

C. Phenytoin 20 mg/kg

D. Call ICU

  1. Same patient, still seizing after lorazepam 4mg IV (given at 5 min). At 15 min:

A. Give levetiracetam

B. Repeat lorazepam 4 mg IV (second benzo dose)

C. Intubate

D. Propofol

  1. A 50-year-old, seizure 30 minutes, received lorazepam x2. Still seizing. Next:

A. Midazolam

B. Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (or phenytoin 20 mg/kg)

C. Phenobarbital

D. Propofol infusion

  1. A 40-year-old on levetiracetam, status epilepticus for 45 minutes, received benzo x2, levetiracetam 60 mg/kg. Still seizing. Next:

A. More levetiracetam

B. Anaesthetic agent (propofol/thiopental) + intubate + ICU

C. Phenytoin

D. Magnesium

  1. A 25-year-old with first seizure, status for 20 min, no IV access. Best first drug:

A. Rectal diazepam

B. Buccal midazolam 10 mg

C. IM phenytoin

D. Oral clonazepam

  1. Non-convulsive status — patient confused, subtle eye movements, no tonic-clonic. Diagnosis:

A. Encephalopathy

B. EEG confirmation (continuous epileptiform activity)

C. Psychogenic

D. Post-ictal

  1. Rhabdomyolysis in status epilepticus — monitoring:

A. Creatinine only

B. CK, electrolytes (K⁺), renal function, urine myoglobin

C. CK only

D. No monitoring needed

  1. Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV — administration:

A. Rapid push

B. Cardiac monitor, 0.9% saline only, max 50 mg/min, avoid extravasation

C. Give in 5% dextrose

D. IM route preferred

  1. A child with febrile status epilepticus >30 min. Management:

A. Different protocol

B. Same time-based protocol (benzo → repeat → phenytoin/levetira → anaesthesia)

C. Only rectal diazepam

D. Observation

  1. Post-status management:

A. Discharge if stopped

B. Maintenance AED, identify/treat trigger, EEG, neuroimaging, admission

C. Stop all AEDs

D. Only follow-up in 1 month

12. Flashcards

  • Q: Status epilepticus definition

A: Continuous seizure >5 min OR recurrent without recovery

  • Q: Time-based protocol

A: 0–5 benzo, 5–20 repeat benzo, 20–40 phenytoin/levetiracetam, >40 anaesthesia+ICU

  • Q: First-line

A: Lorazepam 4mg IV (or midazolam 10mg buccal/IM)

  • Q: Second-line

A: Repeat benzodiazepine ONCE

  • Q: Third-line

A: Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV OR levetiracetam 60mg/kg

  • Q: Fourth-line

A: Anaesthetic agents (propofol/thiopental) + intubation + ICU

  • Q: Always check first

A: Blood glucose (reversible!)

  • Q: Non-convulsive status

A: EEG diagnosis (confusion, subtle movements)

  • Q: Complications

A: Rhabdo, hyperkalaemia, acidosis, aspiration, neuronal damage

  • Q: Refractory status

A: Anaesthetic infusion + continuous EEG + ICU

13. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — >5 min continuous OR recurrent without recovery.

MCQ 2: B — Lorazepam 4mg IV first-line.

MCQ 3: B — Repeat benzo once at 5–20 min.

MCQ 4: B — Phenytoin 20mg/kg OR levetiracetam 60mg/kg.

MCQ 5: C — Lorazepam preferred (longer half-life, less respiratory depression).

MCQ 6: C — Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg.

MCQ 7: C — EEG for non-convulsive status.

MCQ 8: C — Hypertension not typical (usually hypotension from meds/shock).

MCQ 9: B — Propofol/thiopental/midazolam infusion + ICU.

MCQ 10: B — Cardiac monitor, saline only, max 50mg/min, avoid extravasation. Neuronal damage begins 20–30 min.

SBA 1: B — 10 min seizure = status, IV access = lorazepam 4mg IV.

SBA 2: B — 5–20 min = repeat benzo once.

SBA 3: B — 20–40 min = phenytoin/levetiracetam.

SBA 4: B — >40 min = anaesthetic + intubate + ICU.

SBA 5: B — No IV access = buccal midazolam 10mg.

SBA 6: B — Non-convulsive = EEG confirmation.

SBA 7: B — Rhabdo = CK, K⁺, renal, urine myoglobin.

SBA 8: B — Phenytoin: cardiac monitor, saline only, 50mg/min, no extravasation.

SBA 9: B — Same time-based protocol applies to febrile status.

SBA 10: B — Maintenance AED, treat trigger, EEG, imaging, admit.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Toxic Shock Syndromes
topics.html:
Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Distributive Shock]]

[!important]
Toxic shock syndrome (TSS) = acute, life-threatening illness caused by superantigen toxins from S. aureus or S. pyogenes. Rapid recognition and source removal are critical.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise diagnostic criteria for TSS
  • Differentiate staphylococcal vs streptococcal TSS
  • Apply immediate management (antibiotics + IVIG + source control)
  • Know the role of clindamycin as toxin inhibitor

2. Diagnostic Criteria (CDC)

Staphylococcal TSS

  1. Fever ≥38.9°C
  2. Rash (diffuse macular erythroderma)
  3. Desquamation (1–2 weeks later, palms/soles)
  4. Hypotension (SBP ≤90)
  5. ≥3 organ systems involved (GI, muscular, mucosal, renal, hepatic, haematologic, CNS)

Streptococcal TSS (more severe)

  • Isolation of GAS + hypotension + ≥2 organ failures (renal, coagulopathy, hepatic, ARDS, soft tissue necrosis)

3. Aetiology

  • Staphylococcal: TSST-1, enterotoxins — tampons, surgical wounds, nasal packing
  • Streptococcal: SpeA, SpeB — skin/soft tissue infection, varicella, postpartum

4. Management

  1. ICU admission
  2. Aggressive fluid resuscitation (crystalloids)
  3. Broad-spectrum antibiotics: clindamycin + beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam/carbapenem) + vancomycin
  4. Clindamycin — inhibits toxin synthesis (key!)
  5. Source control: remove tampon/drain abscess/explore wound
  6. IVIG 1–2 g/kg (consider in severe/refractory)

5. Red Flags

  • Rapid deterioration
  • ARDS, DIC, renal failure
  • Necrotising fasciitis (surgical emergency)

6. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Clindamycin is essential — inhibits protein (toxin) synthesis
  • IVIG may be lifesaving in severe cases
  • Streptococcal TSS has higher mortality
  • Desquamation is late sign — do not wait for it

7. Common Viva Questions

  1. What are the diagnostic criteria for staphylococcal TSS?
  2. Why is clindamycin preferred in TSS?
  3. What is the role of IVIG?

8. One-Page Revision Summary

TSS = superantigen toxin-mediated shock

Staph (tampons/wounds) vs Strep (soft tissue)

Clindamycin + beta-lactam + vancomycin

Source removal + IVIG + fluids

9. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • CDC criteria for staphylococcal TSS (5 criteria)
  • Streptococcal TSS = GAS + hypotension + ≥2 organ failures
  • Clindamycin inhibits toxin synthesis (key)
  • Source removal essential (tampon, wound, abscess)

Should Know

  • IVIG 1–2 g/kg in severe/refractory
  • Strep TSS higher mortality than Staph
  • Desquamation late sign (1–2 weeks)

Nice to Know

  • Specific toxins: TSST-1, enterotoxins (Staph), SpeA/SpeB (Strep)
  • Necrotising fasciitis as complication
  • Prophylactic clindamycin in surgical packing

10. MCQs (10)

  1. CDC criteria for staphylococcal TSS require how many organ systems involved?

A. 1

B. 2

C. 3

D. 4

  1. Key antibiotic in TSS that inhibits toxin synthesis:

A. Vancomycin

B. Clindamycin

C. Piperacillin-tazobactam

D. Meropenem

  1. Staphylococcal TSS is associated with:

A. TSST-1 and enterotoxins

B. SpeA and SpeB

C. Alpha toxin

D. Panton-Valentine leukocidin

  1. Desquamation in staphylococcal TSS typically occurs:

A. Within 24 hours

B. 1–2 weeks later

C. 3–4 weeks later

D. Never

  1. Streptococcal TSS requires isolation of:

A. S. aureus

B. GAS (Group A Strep)

C. S. pneumoniae

D. S. agalactiae

  1. IVIG dose in severe TSS:

A. 0.5 g/kg

B. 1–2 g/kg

C. 3–4 g/kg

D. 5 g/kg

  1. Most common portal for staphylococcal TSS:

A. Bloodstream

B. Tampons / surgical wounds / nasal packing

C. Respiratory tract

D. Urinary tract

  1. Clindamycin works in TSS by:

A. Killing bacteria rapidly

B. Inhibiting protein (toxin) synthesis

C. Neutralising toxin directly

D. Enhancing phagocytosis

  1. Necrotising fasciitis in TSS requires:

A. Antibiotics only

B. Surgical debridement (emergency)

C. IVIG only

D. Observation

  1. Streptococcal TSS has _____ mortality than staphylococcal TSS.

A. Lower

B. Higher

C. Same

D. Unknown

11. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 24-year-old woman using tampons presents with fever 39.5°C, diffuse rash, hypotension (80/50), vomiting, diarrhoea, and confusion. Most likely diagnosis:

A. Staphylococcal TSS

B. Streptococcal TSS

C. Meningococcal sepsis

D. Anaphylaxis

  1. A 40-year-old man with a surgical wound develops fever, hypotension, and a diffuse rash. Blood cultures grow S. aureus. Antibiotic regimen MUST include:

A. Vancomycin alone

B. Clindamycin + beta-lactam + vancomycin

C. Ceftriaxone alone

D. Metronidazole + ciprofloxacin

  1. A patient with necrotising fasciitis and septic shock. Best management:

A. Antibiotics + IVIG

B. Emergency surgical debridement + antibiotics + ICU

C. Conservative management

D. IVIG alone

  1. Clindamycin is essential in TSS because:

A. It has best bactericidal activity

B. It inhibits toxin synthesis at ribosome level

C. It penetrates abscesses best

D. It covers anaerobes

  1. A 30-year-old woman postpartum develops fever, hypotension, and a painful spreading leg rash. GAS isolated from wound. Diagnosis:

A. Staphylococcal TSS

B. Streptococcal TSS

C. Cellulitis

D. DVT

  1. IVIG in TSS:

A. First-line, before antibiotics

B. Consider in severe/refractory cases (1–2 g/kg)

C. Contraindicated

D. Only for streptococcal TSS

  1. In staphylococcal TSS, desquamation of palms/soles:

A. Early diagnostic sign

B. Occurs 1–2 weeks after onset

C. Never occurs

D. Pathognomonic

  1. Antibiotic covering TSST-1 producing S. aureus:

A. Flucloxacillin

B. Clindamycin + beta-lactam

C. Vancomycin alone

D. Gentamicin

  1. A patient with TSS from nasal packing after rhinoplasty. Source control:

A. Antibiotics only

B. Remove nasal packing

C. IVIG

D. Steroids

  1. Streptococcal TSS organ failures include all EXCEPT:

A. Renal

B. Coagulopathy

C. Hepatic

D. Pancreatitis

  1. Superantigen mechanism:

A. Binds MHC II and TCR non-specifically → massive cytokine release

B. Direct endothelial damage

C. Complement activation

D. Antibody-mediated

12. Flashcards

  • Q: Staph TSS CDC criteria

A: Fever ≥38.9 + rash + desquamation + hypotension + ≥3 organ systems

  • Q: Strep TSS criteria

A: GAS isolation + hypotension + ≥2 organ failures

  • Q: Key antibiotic in TSS

A: Clindamycin (inhibits toxin synthesis)

  • Q: IVIG dose

A: 1–2 g/kg in severe/refractory

  • Q: Desquamation timing

A: 1–2 weeks late sign

  • Q: Staph TSS portals

A: Tampons, surgical wounds, nasal packing

  • Q: Strep TSS portals

A: Skin/soft tissue, varicella, postpartum

13. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: C — ≥3 organ systems.

MCQ 2: B — Clindamycin inhibits protein/toxin synthesis.

MCQ 3: A — TSST-1, enterotoxins.

MCQ 4: B — 1–2 weeks late sign.

MCQ 5: B — GAS (Group A Strep).

MCQ 6: B — 1–2 g/kg.

MCQ 7: B — Tampons, surgical wounds, nasal packing.

MCQ 8: B — Inhibits protein synthesis at 50S ribosome.

MCQ 9: B — Emergency surgical debridement.

MCQ 10: B — Strep TSS higher mortality.

SBA 1: A — Tampon use + fever + rash + hypotension + multi-organ = Staph TSS.

SBA 2: B — Clindamycin (toxin) + beta-lactam + vancomycin (MRSA cover).

SBA 3: B — Necrotising fasciitis = surgical emergency.

SBA 4: B — Clindamycin inhibits toxin synthesis (key mechanism).

SBA 5: B — Postpartum + GAS + shock = Strep TSS.

SBA 6: B — IVIG 1–2 g/kg in severe/refractory.

SBA 7: B — Late sign.

SBA 8: B — Clindamycin + beta-lactam covers TSST-1 producers.

SBA 9: B — Remove packing (source control).

SBA 10: D — Pancreatitis not typical organ failure in Strep TSS.

SBA 11: A — Superantigen binds MHC II + TCR → cytokine storm.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

topics.title: Tracheal Intubation And Rsi In Critically Ill
topics.html:
Related: [[Cardiac Arrest & Post-Resuscitation Care]], [[Sedation, Analgesia and Neuromuscular Blockade in ICU]]

[!important]
Critically ill intubation = high-risk procedure (10-20% complications: hypotension, hypoxia, aspiration, cardiac arrest). DAS 2015 airway assessment + plan A, B, C, D (surgical airway). RSI = Rapid Sequence Induction: preoxygenate (100% O₂ 3-5 min) + induction agent + suxamethonium 1-1.5 mg/kg (or rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg) + intubation. Pre-intubation optimisation ("crash induction"): 500 mL fluid, pre-load, vasopressor ready, NG tube if gastric distension, ramping (obese/pregnant), head-up. Induction agents: propofol (haemodynamic instability concerns), ketamine 1-2 mg/kg (preferred in shock, asthma, sepsis; preserves airway reflexes), etomidate (controversial in sepsis — adrenal suppression), midazolam. Cricoid pressure controversial; BURP (back-up-right-pressure) better. Confirm with waveform capnography + bilateral breath sounds + SpO₂. Plan B: 2nd generation LMA, fibreoptic. Plan C: face mask. Plan D: FONA (front-of-neck access, cricothyroidotomy). FCPS/MRCP: DAS algorithms, RSI drugs, cricoid vs BURP, difficult airway predictors, post-intubation management.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Apply DAS 2015 airway assessment
  • Conduct pre-intubation optimisation
  • Perform RSI with appropriate drug doses
  • Manage difficult airway (Plan A-D)
  • Recognise and manage post-intubation complications
  • Document airway assessment
  • Plan extubation strategy

2. Why Critically Ill Intubation Is High Risk

  • Cardiovascular collapse (30-40% hypotension post-RSI): ↓sympathetic tone + ↓venous return + positive pressure ventilation
  • Hypoxaemia (10-30%): ↑shunt physiology, ↓FRC, aspiration
  • Aspiration risk (full stomach, ileus, gastric distension)
  • Difficult airway (3-5%): anatomical, physiological
  • Cardiac arrest in 1-3% (especially shock)
  • Physiological reserve exhausted

3. Pre-Intubation Checklist (Crash Induction)

Preparation (5-7 minutes)

  • Drugs drawn up + checked
  • Equipment: bag-mask, suction, ETT (7.0-8.0), laryngoscope (Mac + Miller), bougie, supraglottic (LMA/i-gel), 2nd gen LMA
  • Monitoring: SpO₂, ECG, BP (cycling), capnography
  • IV access × 2
  • Suction working (large-bore Yankauer)
  • Plan A-D verbalised
  • Team briefed
  • Pre-oxygenation (3-5 min, 100% O₂ via non-rebreather or NIV/HFNO)

Patient Optimisation

  • IV fluid: 500 mL crystalloid bolus (if no overload risk)
  • Vasopressor ready (noradrenaline)
  • NG tube to decompress stomach
  • Positioning: Ramping (obese, pregnant), head-up 30°
  • Cardiac arrest rescue (CPR plan, difficult airway trolley)
  • NG decompression
  • C-spine precautions if trauma
  • Glycaemic control
  • Anticipate hypotension → give vasopressor early

4. DAS 2015 Airway Assessment

Look-Evaluate-Add-Optimise-Plan-Verify (LEAP-FV)

  • L: Look (facial features, scars)
  • E: Evaluate (3-3-2 rule)
  • A: Add (fentanyl "bulldozer")
  • O: Optimise (positioning, ramping)
  • P: Plan (verbalise Plan A, B, C, D)
  • V: Verify (supraglottic rescue)

3-3-2 Rule

  • 3 fingers mouth opening (>3 cm)
  • 3 fingers hyoid-mental distance (>3 fingers)
  • 2 fingers thyroid-hyoid distance (>2 fingers)

Predictors of Difficult Intubation

CategoryFeatures
AnatomicalShort neck, thick neck, obesity, micrognathia, retrognathia, limited mouth opening, large tongue, prominent teeth, narrow palate
PathologicalTumour, abscess, trauma, oedema, radiation, previous surgery
PhysiologicalHypoxia, shock, agitation
InfectiveEpiglottitis, Ludwig's angina, retropharyngeal abscess
DynamicBleeding, vomiting
DAS 2015 (specific)Restricted neck mobility + reduced mouth opening + jaw protrusion + Mallampati 3-4 + prominent incisors + beard + OSA

Mallampati Score

  • I: Tonsils, uvula, soft palate visible
  • II: Soft palate, partial uvula
  • III: Soft palate only
  • IV: Hard palate only
  • III-IV = difficult intubation

5. Preoxygenation

Methods

  • 100% O₂ via non-rebreather mask × 3-5 min
  • NIV (CPAP/BiPAP) for shunted lungs (ARDS, pulmonary oedema)
  • HFNO (high-flow nasal oxygen) during apnoeic period
  • Bag-mask with 100% O₂ (less effective)

Goals

  • O₂ reserve: SpO₂ >95% (or as high as achievable)
  • Time to desaturation: 8-10 min in healthy; 30-60 s in critically ill
  • End-tidal O₂ >85%

6. RSI Drugs (Doses)

Induction Agents

DrugDoseNotes
Ketamine1-2 mg/kg IVPreferred in shock, sepsis, asthma; preserves airway reflexes, bronchodilator
Propofol0.5-1.5 mg/kg IVReduce dose in shock; hypotension risk
Etomidate0.3 mg/kg IVStable haemodynamics but adrenal suppression (avoid in sepsis)
Midazolam0.15-0.3 mg/kg IVSlower onset, more hypotension, amnestic
Thiopentone3-5 mg/kg IVHistorical; hypotension

NMJ Blockers

DrugDoseOnsetNotes
Suxamethonium1-1.5 mg/kg IV30-60 sDepolarising; contraindicated in burns (>24 h), denervation, hyperK, MH, muscular dystrophy; fasciculations; ↑ICP/↑IOP
Rocuronium1.2 mg/kg IV45-60 sNon-depolarising; rapid; reversible with sugammadex 16 mg/kg
Vecuronium0.1 mg/kg IVSlowerLess used

Adjuncts

  • Fentanyl 1-3 mcg/kg IV (blunt laryngoscopy response, ↓ICP); titrate to haemodynamics
  • Atropine 0.5-1 mg IV (bradycardia, succinylcholine, paediatric)
  • Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg IV (↓ICP, ↓IOP, ↓airway response)

7. RSI Procedure (Step by Step)

Pre-Phase (5-7 min)

  1. Pre-oxygenate 100% O₂ × 3-5 min
  2. IV access × 2
  3. IV fluid bolus 500 mL
  4. NG tube decompression
  5. Position: ramping (obese), head-up 30°
  6. Suction working
  7. Brief team (Plan A-D)
  8. Pre-load drugs: fentanyl, atropine, lidocaine as needed

Induction Phase

  1. Pre-oxygenate final 1-2 min
  2. Induction agent IV push
  3. NMJ blocker IV push (immediately after)
  4. Cricoid pressure (10 N, applied on induction) — controversial (DAS 2015: not routine)
  5. Wait 45-60 s (suxamethonium) or 60-90 s (rocuronium)
  6. Verify paralysis (TOF = 0)

Laryngoscopy Phase

  1. Position (sniffing position)
  2. Laryngoscope (Mac 4 blade standard)
  3. Bougie if needed
  4. BURP (back-up-right-pressure) instead of cricoid
  5. Confirm ETT position:
  • Waveform capnography (mandatory)
  • Bilateral breath sounds
  • SpO₂
  • Depth at teeth (22 cm male, 20 cm female)
  1. Cuff inflated, ETT secured
  2. Cricoid pressure release

Post-Intubation

  1. Continue sedation + analgesia
  2. Lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg PBW, PEEP, plateau <30)
  3. Vasopressor if MAP <65
  4. CXR to confirm ETT position
  5. NG tube to suction
  6. Documentation (grade, ETT size, depth, drugs)

8. DAS 2015 Plan A-D

Plan A: Tracheal Intubation

  • Laryngoscopy (Mac 4 first; video laryngoscope available)
  • Bougie if needed
  • Max 3 attempts (1 operator, 2 operators)
  • Declare failure if: failed within 3 attempts

Plan B: Supraglottic Airway

  • 2nd generation LMA (i-gel, ProSeal, LMA Supreme)
  • Max 3 attempts
  • Don't push if supraglottic not working → Plan C

Plan C: Face Mask Ventilation

  • 2-person technique
  • OPA/NPA
  • Plan for awakening if face mask works
  • Max 2 attempts if no awakening

Plan D: Front-of-Neck Access (FONA)

  • Cricothyroidotomy (scalpel-bougie-tube technique)
  • No time for delay
  • 15 mm ETT 6.0-7.0 or tracheostomy tube
  • Surgical FONA > needle in adults

9. Cricoid Pressure

  • Traditional: Sellick manoeuvre (10-20 N on cricoid)
  • Aim: occlude upper oesophagus, prevent aspiration
  • DAS 2015: not routine, may impede ventilation/laryngoscopy
  • Use only if: cricoid (not thyroid) cartilage, relaxed patient
  • Burp if: distorted view, can't intubate

10. Failed Intubation Drill

  1. Call for help
  2. Declare failure (3 attempts, 3 minutes)
  3. Bag-mask ventilation (2-person, OPA/NPA)
  4. Plan B: LMA
  5. If Plan B fails → Plan C (face mask) or Plan D (FONA)
  6. Consider awakening (reverse NMJ if able)

11. Post-Intubation Complications

ComplicationManagement
Hypotension (30-50%)IV fluid + vasopressor (noradrenaline)
HypoxiaPre-oxygenate, reposition, exclude oesophageal intubation
Oesophageal intubationCapnography, remove, re-intubate
AspirationSuction, antibiotics if needed
ArrhythmiaTreat cause (hypoxia, hyperK)
Cardiac arrestCPR per ALS
Right mainstem intubationPull back, auscultate
PneumothoraxChest drain
Dental traumaManage, document
Sore throatSupportive
Hoarseness/vocal cord paralysisENT referral
Laryngeal oedemaSteroids, extubation care
Tracheal stenosis (late)ENT, surgical

12. Awake Fibreoptic Intubation

  • Indication: known difficult airway, unstable C-spine, oral/pharyngeal cancer
  • Technique: topical anaesthesia (lignocaine 4%) + sedation (remifentanil/midazolam)
  • Equipment: fibrescope, oral/nasal approach
  • Reserve for elective or semi-elective intubation

13. Extubation

  • Plan from start (DAS 2015: extubation strategy)
  • Cuff leak test (see [[Weaning from Mechanical Ventilation]])
  • Postextubation stridor: racemic adrenaline, dexamethasone, Heliox
  • Advanced techniques: extubation to supraglottic (Bailey manoeuvre), staged extubation set
  • Re-intubation if necessary

14. Difficult Airway Society (DAS) Documentation

  • Airway assessment recorded
  • Plan A-D documented
  • Failed attempts: number, technique
  • Final outcome (intubated, FONA, awakened)
  • Follow-up: ICU, surgical airway
  • ICU review by airway-trained specialist

15. Special Situations

Cardiac Arrest

  • No RSI needed — proceed with intubation if skilled
  • Waveform capnography mandatory
  • Drugs: minimal sedation needed

Trauma

  • C-spine immobilisation
  • Aspiration risk (full stomach, blood)
  • Awake fibreoptic if known difficult
  • Surgical airway if orofacial trauma + can't intubate

Pregnancy

  • Reduced FRC (desaturates fast)
  • Aspiration risk (full stomach, ↑progesterone)
  • Left lateral tilt >20 weeks
  • Smaller ETT (6.5-7.0)
  • Pre-oxygenate thoroughly
  • Difficult airway (oedema, weight gain)
  • Ramping mandatory

Sepsis

  • Ketamine preferred (preserves haemodynamics)
  • Avoid etomidate (adrenal suppression)
  • Aggressive fluid + vasopressor
  • Lactate, MAP target

Asthma

  • Ketamine preferred (bronchodilator)
  • RSI, not elective fibreoptic
  • Ventilation strategy: low RR, low Vt, long expiratory time
  • Permissive hypercapnia OK
  • Avoid tube irritation (deep sedation + NMB)

Morbid Obesity

  • Ramping (ear-to-sternal-notch)
  • HFNO + NIV for preoxygenation
  • Difficult airway (BMI >40)
  • Postural (reverse Trendelenburg, head up)
  • Smaller ETT (airway oedema)
  • Difficult ventilation (obstructive, ↓FRC)

16. Paediatric Considerations

  • Narrowest part: cricoid (not vocal cords)
  • Uncuffed below 8 years
  • Age/4 + 4 formula (e.g., 1 y → 1+4 = 4.0, 4 y → 4+4 = 4.0, etc.)
  • Atropine 0.5-1 mg (bradycardia)
  • Suxamethonium OK (no contraindications usually)
  • Difficult airway rare (mostly congenital syndromes)
  • Family presence for non-emergent

17. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  1. Critically ill intubation = high risk (10-20% complications)
  2. DAS 2015 algorithm: Plan A-D (Intubation, Supraglottic, Face mask, FONA)
  3. 3-3-2 rule for airway assessment
  4. Mallampati III-IV = difficult intubation
  5. RSI: preoxygenate + induction + NMJ blocker
  6. Suxamethonium 1-1.5 mg/kg (contraindications: burns >24 h, denervation, hyperK, MH)
  7. Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg (reversible with sugammadex 16 mg/kg)
  8. Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg preferred in shock, sepsis, asthma
  9. Etomidate avoids in sepsis (adrenal suppression)
  10. Cricoid pressure controversial (not routine per DAS 2015)
  11. BURP better for laryngoscopy
  12. Waveform capnography mandatory for confirmation
  13. Pre-intubation optimisation: 500 mL fluid, NG decompression, ramping, vasopressor ready
  14. Pregnancy: left lateral tilt + ramping + smaller ETT
  15. Plan D = cricothyroidotomy (scalpel-bougie-tube)

19. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Capnography mandatory (not auscultation alone)
  • Waveform capnography = oesophageal vs tracheal
  • Suxamethonium contraindicated in burns (>24 h), denervation, hyperK, MH
  • Etomidate avoid in sepsis (single dose → adrenal suppression)
  • Ketamine preferred in shock (preserves sympathetic tone)
  • Propofol ↓BP (avoid in shock at full dose)
  • Cricoid pressure may impair view — use BURP instead
  • Ramping in obese (ear-to-sternal-notch)
  • Pregnancy: left lateral tilt + small ETT + rapid desat
  • Failed intubation = 3 attempts, declare early
  • Plan D = scalpel-bougie-tube FONA (not needle)
  • Sugammadex 16 mg/kg reverses rocuronium
  • Paediatric: age/4 + 4 for ETT, atropine
  • Asthma: ketamine (bronchodilator)

20. Mnemonics

  • 3-3-2 rule: 3 fingers mouth, 3 fingers hyoid-mental, 2 fingers thyroid-hyoid
  • DAS Plan A-D: A tracheal, B supraglottic, C face mask, D FONA
  • RSI: Preox + Induction + NMJ + Wait + Laryngoscopy + Confirm
  • Suxamethonium dose: 1-1.5 mg/kg
  • Rocuronium dose: 1.2 mg/kg
  • Sugammadex: 16 mg/kg for rocuronium
  • Ketamine: 1-2 mg/kg (shock, asthma, sepsis)
  • Pre-intubation: 500 mL fluid, NG, vasopressor, ramping
  • Capnography mandatory
  • BURP not cricoid (routine)
  • C-spine: no sniffing position
  • Pregnancy: left lateral + ramping
  • FONA: scalpel-bougie-tube

24. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Critically ill intubation = high risk (10-20% complications)
  • DAS 2015: Plan A (intubation), Plan B (LMA), Plan C (face mask), Plan D (FONA)
  • 3-3-2 rule for airway assessment; Mallampati III-IV difficult
  • Pre-intubation: preoxygenate 100% O₂ 3-5 min, IV x2, fluid 500 mL, NG, vasopressor, ramping, team brief
  • RSI: preoxygenate + induction + NMJ blocker
  • Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg (preferred in shock, sepsis, asthma)
  • Propofol (avoid in shock at full dose)
  • Etomidate avoid in sepsis (adrenal suppression)
  • Suxamethonium 1-1.5 mg/kg (burns >24 h, denervation, hyperK, MH = NO)
  • Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg (reversible with sugammadex 16 mg/kg)
  • Cricoid pressure controversial (not routine per DAS 2015); BURP better
  • Waveform capnography mandatory for confirmation
  • FONA = scalpel-bougie-tube cricothyroidotomy
  • Pregnancy: left lateral tilt + ramping + small ETT
  • Asthma/sepsis: ketamine preferred

28. Summary

Critically ill intubation = high-risk procedure (10-20% complications: hypotension 30-50%, hypoxia, aspiration, cardiac arrest 1-3%). DAS 2015 algorithm: Plan A tracheal intubation (3 attempts max), Plan B 2nd gen LMA (i-gel, ProSeal), Plan C 2-person face mask, Plan D FONA (front-of-neck access, scalpel-bougie-tube cricothyroidotomy). Airway assessment: 3-3-2 rule (3 fingers mouth opening, 3 fingers hyoid-mental distance, 2 fingers thyroid-hyoid); Mallampati III-IV difficult; look for obesity, beard, OSA, neck mobility. Pre-intubation optimisation ("crash induction"): 100% O₂ × 3-5 min, IV × 2, 500 mL fluid, NG decompression, ramping (obese, pregnant), vasopressor ready, suction working, team brief (Plan A-D). RSI drugs: Induction — ketamine 1-2 mg/kg (preferred in shock, sepsis, asthma), propofol 0.5-1.5 mg/kg (avoid in shock at full dose), etomidate 0.3 mg/kg (avoid in sepsis — adrenal suppression); NMJ — suxamethonium 1-1.5 mg/kg (contraindicated in burns >24 h, denervation, hyperK, MH, muscular dystrophy) or rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg (reversible with sugammadex 16 mg/kg). Cricoid pressure controversial (not routine per DAS 2015); BURP (back-up-right-pressure) preferred. Waveform capnography mandatory for ETT confirmation (not auscultation alone). Pregnancy: left lateral tilt >20 weeks, ramping, smaller ETT (6.5-7.0), rapid desaturation. Asthma: ketamine (bronchodilator), deep sedation + NMB. Sepsis: ketamine, avoid etomidate, vasopressor ready. Morbid obesity: ramping, HFNO/NIV preoxygenation, smaller ETT. Post-intubation: lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg PBW, PEEP, plateau <30), sedation, vasopressor, CXR.
